summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Physical Quantities & Units

Physical Quantities

A physical quantity is any property of a material or system that can be measured. Every measurement has two parts: a numerical value and a unit.

  • Base Quantities: The seven fundamental quantities defined by the SI system — length (m), mass (kg), time (s), electric current (A), temperature (K), amount of substance (mol), and luminous intensity (cd).
  • Derived Quantities: Quantities formed by combining base quantities through multiplication or division — e.g., speed (m/s), force (kg·m/s² = N), energy (kg·m²/s² = J).

Why Units Matter

  • Units ensure measurements are meaningful and comparable. A number without a unit (e.g., "5") has no physical meaning.
  • Consistent units prevent errors in calculations — always convert to SI before computing.
  • Unit analysis can help verify that an equation is dimensionally correct.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: SI System & Prefixes

The International System of Units (SI)

The SI system is the globally adopted standard for scientific measurement. It defines seven base units from which all other units are derived.

  • Meter (m) — length
  • Kilogram (kg) — mass
  • Second (s) — time
  • Ampere (A) — electric current
  • Kelvin (K) — temperature
  • Mole (mol) — amount of substance
  • Candela (cd) — luminous intensity

SI Prefixes

Prefixes scale units by powers of 10 for convenience:

  • Giga (G) = 10⁹, Mega (M) = 10⁶, Kilo (k) = 10³
  • Centi (c) = 10⁻², Milli (m) = 10⁻³, Micro (μ) = 10⁻⁶, Nano (n) = 10⁻⁹

Example: 5.2 km = 5.2 × 10³ m = 5200 m

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the prefix 'kilo' mean?
quiz_questions.explanation: SI prefixes: kilo (10³), centi (10⁻²), milli (10⁻³), micro (10⁻⁶), nano (10⁻⁹), pico (10⁻¹²), mega (10⁶), giga (10⁹). The answer is '10³ (one thousand)'. The other options — '10⁶ (one million)', '10⁻³ (one thousandth)', and '10² (one hundred)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The other options — '10⁻⁶ (one millionth)', '10³ (one thousand)', and '10⁻² (one hundredth)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The answer is '5200 m'. The other options — '52 m', '520 m', and '0.52 m' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Scalars vs Vectors
summary_sections.content_html:
Scalar Quantities

Scalars have only magnitude (size). They are fully described by a number and a unit.

  • Examples: mass (5 kg), temperature (300 K), speed (10 m/s), energy (50 J), time (3 s).

Vector Quantities

Vectors have both magnitude and direction. They are represented by arrows.

  • Examples: displacement (5 m east), velocity (10 m/s north), force (20 N downward), acceleration (9.8 m/s² down).

Key Differences

  • Scalars are added arithmetically: 3 kg + 2 kg = 5 kg.
  • Vectors must be added using vector addition (tip-to-tail or component method). Two 3 N forces at right angles give √(9+9) = 3√2 ≈ 4.24 N, not 6 N.
  • Distance is a scalar; displacement is a vector. Speed is a scalar; velocity is a vector.

quiz_questions.explanation: Scalar Quantities Scalars have only magnitude (size). The other options — 'A quantity with magnitude and direction', 'A quantity with no units', and 'A quantity that only describes direction' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a vector quantity?
quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line distance from start to finish with direction (vector). It differs from distance, which is the total path length (scalar). The answer is 'Displacement'. The other options — 'Speed', 'Mass', and 'Temperature' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is speed a scalar or vector?
quiz_questions.explanation: Vectors have both magnitude and direction (velocity, force, acceleration). Scalars have only magnitude (speed, mass, energy). Vectors can be decomposed into perpendicular components. The answer is 'Scalar'. The other options — 'Vector', 'Both', and 'Neither' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is velocity a scalar or vector?
quiz_questions.explanation: Vectors have both magnitude and direction (velocity, force, acceleration). Scalars have only magnitude (speed, mass, energy). Vectors can be decomposed into perpendicular components. The answer is 'Vector'. The other options — 'Scalar', 'Both', and 'Neither' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Vectors must be added using vector addition (tip-to-tail or component method). The other options — 'By simple arithmetic addition', 'By multiplying their magnitudes', and 'By subtracting the smaller from the larger' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Examples — mass (5 kg), temperature (300 K), speed (10 m/s), energy (50 J), time (3 s). The other options — 'Force', 'Velocity', and 'Displacement' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Accuracy, Precision, & Significant Figures

Accuracy vs Precision

  • Accuracy: How close a measurement is to the true (accepted) value. High accuracy = small systematic error.
  • Precision: How close repeated measurements are to each other. High precision = small random error.
  • A measurement can be precise but not accurate (consistently wrong), or accurate but not precise (scattered around the true value).

Significant Figures

  • All nonzero digits are significant: 123 has 3 sig figs.
  • Zeros between nonzero digits are significant: 1002 has 4 sig figs.
  • Leading zeros are NOT significant: 0.0045 has 2 sig figs.
  • Trailing zeros after a decimal point ARE significant: 2.50 has 3 sig figs.

Rules for Calculations

  • Multiplication/Division: Result has the same number of sig figs as the factor with the fewest.
  • Addition/Subtraction: Result is rounded to the same decimal place as the least precise input.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Dimensional Analysis
summary_sections.content_html:
What is Dimensional Analysis?

Dimensional analysis is a technique that uses the dimensions (units) of physical quantities to check equations, convert units, and derive relationships.

  • Every term in a valid physics equation must have the same dimensions on both sides.
  • The three fundamental dimensions are: [M] (mass), [L] (length), [T] (time).

Using Dimensional Analysis

  • Checking equations: Verify that F = ma is dimensionally correct → [M][L][T]⁻² = [M] · [L][T]⁻² ✓
  • Unit conversion: Use conversion factors as fractions equal to 1. E.g., 60 mph × (1609 m / 1 mi) × (1 hr / 3600 s) ≈ 26.8 m/s.
  • Deriving formulas: If the period T of a pendulum depends on length L and g, then T ∝ √(L/g) by dimensional reasoning.

Limitations: Dimensional analysis cannot determine dimensionless constants (like 2π) or distinguish between quantities with the same dimensions (work vs torque).

quiz_questions.explanation: Dimensional analysis uses base quantities [M] (mass), [L] (length), [T] (time). Velocity: [L][T]⁻¹. Acceleration: [L][T]⁻². Force: [M][L][T]⁻². Energy: [M][L]²[T]⁻². The answer is '[L][T]⁻¹'. The other options — '[M][L][T]⁻²', '[L]²[T]⁻¹', and '[M][T]⁻¹' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The answer is '[M][L][T]⁻²'. The other options — '[M][L][T]⁻¹', '[L][T]⁻²', and '[M][L]²[T]⁻²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Limitations — Dimensional analysis cannot determine dimensionless constants (like 2π) or distinguish between quantities with the same dimensions (work vs torque). summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Measurement Uncertainty & Error Analysis

Types of Errors

  • Systematic Errors: Consistent, repeatable errors that shift all measurements in the same direction. Caused by faulty equipment, calibration issues, or flawed technique. They affect accuracy.
  • Random Errors: Unpredictable fluctuations that cause measurements to scatter around the true value. Caused by limitations of the instrument or observer. They affect precision.

Expressing Uncertainty

  • Absolute Uncertainty: The ± range of a measurement. E.g., 5.0 ± 0.1 cm.
  • Percentage Uncertainty: (absolute uncertainty / measured value) × 100%. E.g., (0.1/5.0) × 100% = 2%.

Propagation of Uncertainties

  • Addition/Subtraction: Add absolute uncertainties. If A = 5.0 ± 0.1 and B = 3.0 ± 0.2, then A + B = 8.0 ± 0.3.
  • Multiplication/Division: Add percentage uncertainties.
  • Powers: Multiply the percentage uncertainty by the power. If x has 2% uncertainty, x³ has 6% uncertainty.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a systematic error?

options.text: A consistent error that shifts all measurements in the same direction
An error caused by the observer
An error that averages out over many trials

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Measurement Uncertainty & Error Analysis Types of Errors Systematic Errors : Consistent, repeatable errors that shift all measurements in the same direction. The other options — 'A random fluctuation in measurements', 'An error caused by the observer', and 'An error that averages out over many trials' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Random errors affect which property?
quiz_questions.explanation: They affect precision. Random Errors — Unpredictable fluctuations that cause measurements to scatter around the true value. The other options — 'Accuracy', 'Units', and 'Dimensions' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you calculate percentage uncertainty?
quiz_questions.explanation: Percentage Uncertainty — (absolute uncertainty / measured value) × 100%. The other options — '(Measured value / absolute uncertainty) × 100%', 'Absolute uncertainty × measured value', and 'Absolute uncertainty − measured value' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do uncertainties combine in addition?
quiz_questions.explanation: Propagation of Uncertainties Addition/Subtraction : Add absolute uncertainties. Multiplication/Division : Add percentage uncertainties. The other options — 'Add the percentage uncertainties', 'Multiply the uncertainties', and 'Take the larger uncertainty' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do uncertainties combine in multiplication?
quiz_questions.explanation: Cations are positive ions (formed by losing electrons); anions are negative ions (formed by gaining electrons). Metals typically form cations; nonmetals form anions. The answer is 'Add the percentage uncertainties'. The other options — 'Add the absolute uncertainties', 'Multiply the absolute uncertainties', and 'Subtract the uncertainties' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If x has 2% uncertainty, what is the uncertainty in x³?
quiz_questions.explanation: If x has 2% uncertainty, x³ has 6% uncertainty. The other options — '2%', '8%', and '3%' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Systematic errors affect which property?
quiz_questions.explanation: They affect accuracy. Key Concepts — Measurement Uncertainty & Error Analysis Types of Errors Systematic Errors : Consistent, repeatable errors that shift all measurements in the same direction. The other options — 'Precision', 'Significant figures', and 'Sample size' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Electric Charge & Coulomb’s Law

Electric Charge

  • Two types: positive (protons) and negative (electrons). Like charges repel; unlike attract.
  • Charge is quantized: comes in multiples of e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.
  • Charge is conserved: total charge in an isolated system stays constant.

Coulomb's Law

F = kq₁q₂/r²

  • k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C² (Coulomb's constant).
  • Force is along the line joining the charges.
  • Inverse-square law: double the distance → force becomes ¼.

Charging Methods

  • Friction: Rubbing transfers electrons between materials.
  • Conduction: Direct contact with a charged object.
  • Induction: Rearranging charges by bringing a charged object nearby without contact.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Coulomb's Law.
quiz_questions.explanation: Coulomb's Law: F = kq₁q₂/r², where k = 8.99×10⁹ N·m²/C². Like charges repel; opposite charges attract. Force varies inversely with distance squared. The answer is 'F = kq₁q₂/r²'. The other options — 'F = kq₁q₂/r', 'F = kq₁q₂r²', and 'F = q₁q₂/r²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The answer is 'Force decreases by a factor of 4'. The other options — 'Force halves', 'Force doubles', and 'Force quadruples' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Charge is quantized — comes in multiples of e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C. The other options — 'No — charge is continuous', 'Only for electrons', and 'Only in metals' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Electric Field & Potential

Electric Field

  • A region where a charged particle experiences a force. E = F/q = kQ/r².
  • Direction: away from positive charges, toward negative charges.
  • Unit: N/C or V/m.

Electric Potential (Voltage)

  • Electric potential is the energy per unit charge: V = U/q = kQ/r.
  • Potential difference (voltage): ΔV = W/q — work done per unit charge moving between two points.
  • Unit: Volt (V). 1 V = 1 J/C.

Equipotential Lines

  • Lines connecting points of equal potential. Always perpendicular to field lines.
  • No work is done moving a charge along an equipotential.

Relationship: E = −dV/dr. The field points in the direction of decreasing potential.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an electric field?

options.text: A region where a charged particle experiences a force
A magnetic field around a wire
The space inside a capacitor only

quiz_questions.explanation: An electric field E exerts force on charges: F = qE. The field points away from positive charges and toward negative charges. Field strength: E = kQ/r² (point charge). The answer is 'A region where a charged particle experiences a force'. The other options — 'A region with no charges', 'A magnetic field around a wire', and 'The space inside a capacitor only' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for electric field?

The answer is 'E = F/q = kQ/r²'. The other options — 'E = Fq', 'E = kQ/r', and 'E = FR²/q' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Capacitance
summary_sections.content_html:
Capacitors

  • A capacitor stores electrical energy in an electric field between two conductors (plates).
  • Capacitance: C = Q/V — the ratio of charge stored to voltage applied. Unit: Farad (F).

Parallel Plate Capacitor

  • C = ε₀A/d, where A = plate area, d = plate separation, ε₀ = 8.85 × 10⁻¹² F/m.
  • Inserting a dielectric material (κ) between plates increases capacitance: C = κε₀A/d.

Energy Stored

  • E = ½CV² = ½QV = Q²/(2C).

Combinations

  • Parallel: C_total = C₁ + C₂ + ... (add directly).
  • Series: 1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ + ... (reciprocals add).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a capacitor store?

options.text: Electrical energy in an electric field

quiz_questions.explanation: Capacitance C = Q/V measures a capacitor's ability to store charge. For parallel plates: C = ε₀A/d. Energy stored: U = ½CV². The answer is 'Electrical energy in an electric field'. The other options — 'Magnetic energy', 'Charge only', and 'Current' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Capacitance C = Q/V measures a capacitor's ability to store charge. For parallel plates: C = ε₀A/d. Energy stored: U = ½CV². The answer is 'C = Q/V'. The other options — 'C = V/Q', 'C = QV', and 'C = Q²/V' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Capacitance C = Q/V measures a capacitor's ability to store charge. For parallel plates: C = ε₀A/d. Energy stored: U = ½CV². The answer is 'Farad (F)'. The other options — 'Coulomb (C)', 'Volt (V)', and 'Henry (H)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does inserting a dielectric affect capacitance?

The other options — 'Decreases it', 'No effect', and 'Makes it zero' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do capacitors combine in parallel?
quiz_questions.explanation: Capacitance C = Q/V measures a capacitor's ability to store charge. For parallel plates: C = ε₀A/d. Energy stored: U = ½CV². The answer is 'C_total = C₁ + C₂'. The other options — '1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂', 'C_total = C₁ × C₂', and 'C_total = C₁ − C₂' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The other options — 'E = CV', 'E = C²V', and 'E = 2CV²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Capacitance C = Q/V measures a capacitor's ability to store charge. For parallel plates: C = ε₀A/d. Energy stored: U = ½CV². The answer is 'Doubles'. summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Current, Resistance, & Ohm’s Law

Electric Current

  • Current is the rate of flow of charge: I = Q/t. Unit: Ampere (A). 1 A = 1 C/s.
  • Conventional current flows from + to − (opposite to electron flow).

Resistance

  • Resistance opposes current flow: R = ρL/A, where ρ = resistivity, L = length, A = cross-sectional area.
  • Unit: Ohm (Ω). Resistivity depends on material and temperature.

Ohm's Law

V = IR

  • The voltage across a resistor equals current times resistance.
  • Ohm's law applies to ohmic materials — those with constant resistance (e.g., metals at constant temperature).
  • Non-ohmic: diodes, filament bulbs (resistance changes with current/temperature).

Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is electric current?

options.text: Rate of flow of charge: I = Q/t

Conventional current flows from + to − (opposite to electron flow). The other options — 'Charge times time', 'Voltage divided by power', and 'Resistance times voltage' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Ohm's Law states V = IR (voltage = current × resistance). Rearrange to find the unknown quantity. The answer is V = IR. The other options — 'V = I/R', 'V = I²R', and 'V = IR²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: DC Circuits & Kirchhoff’s Laws

Series Circuits

  • Same current through all components: I_total = I₁ = I₂.
  • Voltages add: V_total = V₁ + V₂.
  • Resistances add: R_total = R₁ + R₂.

Parallel Circuits

  • Same voltage across all branches: V_total = V₁ = V₂.
  • Currents add: I_total = I₁ + I₂.
  • 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂.

Kirchhoff's Laws

  • Junction Rule (KCL): Total current into a junction = total current out. (Conservation of charge.)
  • Loop Rule (KVL): The sum of all voltage gains and drops around any closed loop = 0. (Conservation of energy.)

Problem-Solving

  • Assign current directions (guess if needed — a negative answer means opposite direction).
  • Write KCL for junctions and KVL for loops. Solve the system of equations.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a series circuit, what stays the same?
quiz_questions.explanation: In a series circuit, components share one path for current. Current is the same throughout; voltage divides across components. Total resistance adds: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + ... The answer is 'Current'. The other options — 'Voltage', 'Resistance', and 'Power' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a parallel circuit, what stays the same?
quiz_questions.explanation: In a parallel circuit, components have multiple paths. Voltage is the same across all branches; current divides. 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + ... The answer is 'Voltage across all branches'. The other options — 'Current through all branches', 'Resistance', and 'Power' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Resistors in series: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + ... (currents equal, voltages add). In parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + ... (voltages equal, currents add). The answer is 'R_total = R₁ + R₂'. The other options — '1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂', 'R_total = R₁ × R₂', and 'R_total = R₁ − R₂' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Magnetic Fields & Forces

Magnetic Fields

  • Produced by moving charges (currents) and permanent magnets.
  • Field lines go from North to South outside the magnet.
  • Unit: Tesla (T). Earth's field ≈ 5 × 10⁻⁵ T.

Force on a Moving Charge

  • F = qvB sin θ — force is perpendicular to both velocity and field (use right-hand rule).
  • A stationary charge experiences no magnetic force.
  • The magnetic force does no work (it changes direction, not speed).

Force on a Current-Carrying Wire

  • F = BIL sin θ — force on a wire of length L carrying current I in field B.

Right-Hand Rule: Point fingers in the direction of velocity (or current), curl toward B — thumb points in the direction of force (for positive charges).

quiz_questions.question_text: What produces magnetic fields?
quiz_questions.explanation: A magnetic force acts on moving charges: F = qv × B. The direction follows the right-hand rule. Current-carrying wires create magnetic fields and experience forces in external fields. The answer is 'Moving charges (currents) and permanent magnets'. The other options — 'Static charges only', 'Gravity', and 'Sound waves' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of magnetic field?
quiz_questions.explanation: A magnetic force acts on moving charges: F = qv × B. The direction follows the right-hand rule. Current-carrying wires create magnetic fields and experience forces in external fields. The answer is 'Tesla (T)'. The other options — 'Newton (N)', 'Henry (H)', and 'Weber (Wb)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does a stationary charge experience a magnetic force?
quiz_questions.explanation: Coulomb's Law: F = kq₁q₂/r², where k = 8.99×10⁹ N·m²/C². Like charges repel; opposite charges attract. Force varies inversely with distance squared. The answer is 'No'. The other options — 'Yes', 'Only in strong fields', and 'Only near magnets' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Coulomb's Law: F = kq₁q₂/r², where k = 8.99×10⁹ N·m²/C². Like charges repel; opposite charges attract. Force varies inversely with distance squared. The answer is 'F = qvB sin θ'. The other options — 'F = qvB cos θ', 'F = qB/v', and 'F = qv/B' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does the magnetic force do work on a moving charge?
quiz_questions.explanation: Coulomb's Law: F = kq₁q₂/r², where k = 8.99×10⁹ N·m²/C². Like charges repel; opposite charges attract. Force varies inversely with distance squared. The answer is 'No — it changes direction only'. The other options — 'Yes', 'Only at high speeds', and 'Only for electrons' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: A magnetic force acts on moving charges: F = qv × B. The direction follows the right-hand rule. Current-carrying wires create magnetic fields and experience forces in external fields. The answer is 'Both v and B'. The other options — 'Only v', 'Only B', and 'Neither' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: A magnetic force acts on moving charges: F = qv × B. The direction follows the right-hand rule. Current-carrying wires create magnetic fields and experience forces in external fields. The answer is 'North to South'. The other options — 'South to North', 'East to West', and 'They don't have direction' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Electromagnetic Induction
summary_sections.content_html:
Faraday's Law

  • A changing magnetic flux through a loop induces an EMF (voltage).
  • EMF = −dΦ/dt (Faraday's law). Φ = BA cos θ (magnetic flux).
  • The induced EMF drives a current in a closed circuit.

Lenz's Law

  • The induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change in flux that caused it.
  • This is the reason for the negative sign in Faraday's law — it ensures energy conservation.

Ways to Induce EMF

  • Change the magnetic field strength (B).
  • Change the area of the loop (A).
  • Change the angle between the loop and the field (θ).
  • Move a conductor through a magnetic field.

Applications: Generators, transformers, induction cooktops, wireless charging.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Faraday's Law.
quiz_questions.explanation: EMF = −dΦ/dt (Faraday's law). Φ = BA cos θ (magnetic flux). Faraday's Law A changing magnetic flux through a loop induces an EMF (voltage). The other options — 'EMF = Φ/t', 'EMF = BIL', and 'EMF = IR' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: EMF = −dΦ/dt (Faraday's law). Φ = BA cos θ (magnetic flux). Faraday's Law A changing magnetic flux through a loop induces an EMF (voltage). The other options — 'Φ = BA sin θ', 'Φ = B/A', and 'Φ = BA²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

options.text: Induced current opposes the change in flux that caused it

The other options — 'Induced current aids the change in flux', 'There is no induced current', and 'Current always flows clockwise' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The answer is 'Generators and transformers'. The other options — 'Batteries and resistors', 'Capacitors and inductors', and 'Magnets and compasses' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Lenz's Law The induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change in flux that caused it. This is the reason for the negative sign in Faraday's law — it ensures energy conservation. The other options — 'It's a mathematical convention only', 'EMF is always negative', and 'Flux is always negative' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Alternating Current (AC) Circuits
summary_sections.content_html:
Alternating Current

  • AC current and voltage vary sinusoidally: V = V₀ sin(ωt), I = I₀ sin(ωt).
  • RMS values: V_rms = V₀/√2, I_rms = I₀/√2. These are the equivalent DC values for power calculations.

AC Circuit Components

  • Resistor: V and I are in phase. P = I²R.
  • Capacitor: Current leads voltage by 90°. Reactance: X_C = 1/(ωC).
  • Inductor: Voltage leads current by 90°. Reactance: X_L = ωL.

Impedance

  • Z = √(R² + (X_L − X_C)²) — total opposition to AC.
  • Resonance: When X_L = X_C (ω = 1/√(LC)), impedance is minimum (Z = R) and current is maximum.

Transformers: V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁. Step-up (N₂ > N₁) increases voltage; step-down decreases it.

The other options — 'DC resistance only', 'Capacitive reactance only', and 'Inductive reactance only' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The answer is 'Impedance is minimum (Z = R), current is maximum'. The other options — 'Impedance is maximum', 'Current is zero', and 'Voltage is zero' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Step-up (N₂ > N₁) increases voltage; step-down decreases it. N₁) increases voltage; step-down decreases it."> Transformers : V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁. summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Maxwell’s Equations (Introductory)

Maxwell's Four Equations

These equations unify electricity and magnetism and predict electromagnetic waves.

  • Gauss's Law (Electric): Electric flux through a closed surface ∝ enclosed charge. ∮E·dA = Q/ε₀.
  • Gauss's Law (Magnetic): No magnetic monopoles exist. ∮B·dA = 0 (field lines always form closed loops).
  • Faraday's Law: A changing magnetic field induces an electric field. ∮E·dl = −dΦ_B/dt.
  • Ampère-Maxwell Law: Magnetic fields are produced by currents and by changing electric fields. ∮B·dl = μ₀I + μ₀ε₀(dΦ_E/dt).

Electromagnetic Waves

  • Maxwell showed that changing E and B fields propagate as waves at speed c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀) ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s.
  • This predicted that light is an electromagnetic wave — later confirmed by Hertz.

The other options — 'Magnetic charges exist', 'Electric fields are zero', and 'Gravity determines magnetism' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.
