quiz_questions.explanation: This predicted that light is an electromagnetic wave — later confirmed by Hertz. The other options — 'Isaac Newton', 'Albert Einstein', and 'Michael Faraday' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What unification did Maxwell achieve?
quiz_questions.explanation: Cations are positive ions (formed by losing electrons); anions are negative ions (formed by gaining electrons). Metals typically form cations; nonmetals form anions. The answer is 'He unified electricity and magnetism'. The other options — 'He unified gravity and light', 'He unified mass and energy', and 'He unified sound and heat' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Photoelectric Effect
summary_sections.content_html:
The Photoelectric Effect

  • Light shining on a metal surface can eject electrons — these are called photoelectrons.
  • This effect could not be explained by classical wave theory.

Key Observations

  • There is a threshold frequency (f₀) below which no electrons are emitted, regardless of intensity.
  • Above f₀, increasing intensity increases the number of electrons but not their maximum KE.
  • Increasing frequency increases the maximum KE of emitted electrons.
  • Emission is instantaneous — no time delay.

Einstein's Explanation

  • Light consists of photons, each with energy E = hf.
  • KE_max = hf − φ, where φ = hf₀ is the work function (minimum energy to free an electron).
  • Below the threshold frequency, no single photon has enough energy to eject an electron.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the photoelectric effect?

options.text: Light ejects electrons from a metal surface

quiz_questions.explanation: The Photoelectric Effect Light shining on a metal surface can eject electrons — these are called photoelectrons. The other options — 'Light heats up metal', 'Electrons create light', and 'Metal absorbs all light' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What increases the maximum KE of photoelectrons?
quiz_questions.explanation: Increasing frequency increases the maximum KE of emitted electrons. The Photoelectric Effect Light shining on a metal surface can eject electrons — these are called photoelectrons. The other options — 'Increasing the intensity below threshold', 'Decreasing the frequency', and 'Making the metal thicker' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is Einstein's photoelectric equation?
quiz_questions.explanation: KE_max = hf − φ , where φ = hf₀ is the work function (minimum energy to free an electron). The other options — 'KE = ½mv²', 'E = mc²', and 'KE = hf + φ' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the work function (φ)?

options.text: Minimum energy to free an electron from the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: KE_max = hf − φ , where φ = hf₀ is the work function (minimum energy to free an electron). The other options — 'Maximum energy of an electron', 'The speed of light', and 'Planck's constant' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is emission of photoelectrons instantaneous?

options.text: No — there is always a delay

quiz_questions.explanation: Emission is instantaneous — no time delay. The other options — 'No — there is always a delay', 'Only for intense light', and 'Only for UV light' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why couldn't classical wave theory explain the photoelectric effect?

options.text: It predicted any frequency would work with enough intensity

quiz_questions.explanation: The Photoelectric Effect Light shining on a metal surface can eject electrons — these are called photoelectrons. This effect could not be explained by classical wave theory. The other options — 'It predicted the correct results', 'Classical theory doesn't discuss light', and 'It predicted electrons are waves' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does increasing intensity below threshold frequency eject electrons?
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Observations There is a threshold frequency (f₀) below which no electrons are emitted, regardless of intensity. Above f₀, increasing intensity increases the number of electrons but not their maximum KE. The other options — 'Yes', 'Only with enough time', and 'Only at low temperatures' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Atomic Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Historical Atomic Models

  • Thomson's "Plum Pudding": Positive charge spread uniformly with electrons embedded in it.
  • Rutherford's Nuclear Model: Most of the atom is empty space; mass and positive charge concentrated in a tiny nucleus. Discovered via gold foil experiment.
  • Bohr Model: Electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed energy levels (n = 1, 2, 3...). Electrons can only gain or lose energy by jumping between levels.
  • Quantum Mechanical Model: Electrons exist in probability clouds (orbitals), not definite paths.

Bohr Model Details

  • Energy levels: E_n = −13.6/n² eV (for hydrogen).
  • Photon emission: ΔE = hf = E_upper − E_lower.
  • Spectral lines correspond to transitions between specific energy levels.

Line Spectra

  • Emission spectrum: Bright lines from hot gas — electrons dropping to lower levels.
  • Absorption spectrum: Dark lines where light is absorbed — electrons jumping to higher levels.

quiz_questions.question_text: What experiment led to Rutherford's model?
quiz_questions.explanation: Discovered via gold foil experiment. Rutherford's Nuclear Model : Most of the atom is empty space; mass and positive charge concentrated in a tiny nucleus. The other options — 'The double-slit experiment', 'The photoelectric effect', and 'Thomson's cathode ray experiment' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What did Rutherford discover?

options.text: Most of the atom is empty space with a tiny dense nucleus

quiz_questions.explanation: The nucleus houses the cell's DNA and controls gene expression. It is surrounded by a double membrane (nuclear envelope) with nuclear pores for transport. The answer is 'Most of the atom is empty space with a tiny dense nucleus'. The other options — 'Atoms are solid spheres', 'Electrons orbit randomly', and 'Atoms have no nucleus' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the Bohr model, electrons move in:
quiz_questions.explanation: Bohr Model — Electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed energy levels (n = 1, 2, 3..). Bohr Model Details Energy levels: E_n = −13.6/n² eV (for hydrogen). The other options — 'Random paths', 'Straight lines', and 'Spirals into the nucleus' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy of the nth level in hydrogen:
quiz_questions.explanation: Bohr Model Details Energy levels: E_n = −13.6/n² eV (for hydrogen). The other options — 'E_n = −13.6n² eV', 'E_n = 13.6/n eV', and 'E_n = n × 13.6 eV' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an emission spectrum?

options.text: Bright lines from electrons dropping to lower levels
Dark lines in a continuous spectrum

quiz_questions.explanation: Line Spectra Emission spectrum : Bright lines from hot gas — electrons dropping to lower levels. Absorption spectrum — Dark lines where light is absorbed — electrons jumping to higher levels. The other options — 'Dark lines in a continuous spectrum', 'A continuous rainbow', and 'X-ray radiation' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What describes electrons in the quantum mechanical model?
quiz_questions.explanation: Quantum mechanics describes matter and energy at the atomic scale. Photon energy E = hf. De Broglie wavelength λ = h/mv relates particle momentum to wave behavior. The answer is 'Probability clouds (orbitals)'. The other options — 'Definite circular orbits', 'Straight line paths', and 'Fixed positions' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Spectral lines are evidence of:
quiz_questions.explanation: Spectral lines correspond to transitions between specific energy levels. Bohr Model — Electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed energy levels (n = 1, 2, 3..). The other options — 'Continuous energy', 'Classical orbits', and 'Wave-particle duality' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Nuclear Physics
summary_sections.content_html:
Nuclear Structure

  • Nucleus contains protons (positive) and neutrons (neutral), collectively called nucleons.
  • Atomic number Z = number of protons. Mass number A = protons + neutrons.
  • Isotopes: Same Z, different number of neutrons.

Radioactive Decay

  • Alpha (α): Emits ⁴₂He. Z decreases by 2, A decreases by 4.
  • Beta (β⁻): A neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino. Z increases by 1.
  • Gamma (γ): Emission of a high-energy photon. No change in Z or A.
  • Half-life: Time for half the nuclei to decay. N = N₀(½)^(t/t½).

Nuclear Reactions

  • Fission: A heavy nucleus splits into lighter nuclei, releasing energy (nuclear reactors, atomic bombs).
  • Fusion: Light nuclei combine to form a heavier nucleus, releasing energy (stars, hydrogen bombs).
  • Both release energy due to mass defect: E = Δmc² (Einstein's mass-energy equivalence).

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the two types of particles in a nucleus?
quiz_questions.explanation: Neutrons are neutral (uncharged) subatomic particles in the nucleus. Isotopes of an element differ in the number of neutrons, which affects mass but not chemical properties. The answer is 'Protons and neutrons'. The other options — 'Electrons and protons', 'Quarks and leptons', and 'Photons and neutrons' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the atomic number Z?

options.text: The number of electrons in an ion

quiz_questions.explanation: The atomic number (Z) equals the number of protons in the nucleus. It defines the element's identity and determines its position on the periodic table. The answer is 'The number of protons'. The other options — 'The number of neutrons', 'The total nucleons', and 'The number of electrons in an ion' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are isotopes?

options.text: Atoms with the same Z but different neutrons

quiz_questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons (same atomic number, different mass number). They have identical chemical properties but different masses. The answer is 'Atoms with the same Z but different neutrons'. The other options — 'Atoms with different Z', 'Atoms with no neutrons', and 'Different elements with same mass' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is alpha decay?

options.text: Emission of a ⁴₂He nucleus; Z decreases by 2, A by 4

quiz_questions.explanation: Alpha particles (⁴₂He) are heavy (+2 charge) and can be stopped by paper or skin. In alpha decay, the parent loses 2 protons and 2 neutrons (atomic number -2, mass number -4). The answer is 'Emission of a ⁴₂He nucleus; Z decreases by 2, A by 4'. The other options — 'Emission of an electron', 'Emission of a photon', and 'Emission of a neutron' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is half-life?

options.text: Time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
Time for all nuclei to decay
Time for one nucleus to form

quiz_questions.explanation: Half-life is the time for half the substance to decay/react. After n half-lives, the remaining amount = initial × (1/2)ⁿ. The answer is Time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay. The other options — 'Time for all nuclei to decay', 'Time for one nucleus to form', and 'Time for radiation to stop' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is nuclear fission?

options.text: A heavy nucleus splitting into lighter nuclei

quiz_questions.explanation: Nuclear fission splits a heavy nucleus (like uranium-235) into lighter nuclei, releasing neutrons and energy. A chain reaction occurs when released neutrons trigger more fissions. The answer is 'A heavy nucleus splitting into lighter nuclei'. The other options — 'Light nuclei combining', 'Any nuclear reaction', and 'Radioactive decay only' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is nuclear fusion?

options.text: Light nuclei combining into a heavier nucleus

quiz_questions.explanation: Nuclear fusion combines light nuclei (like hydrogen) into heavier ones, releasing energy. It requires extremely high temperatures (millions of degrees) to overcome electrostatic repulsion. The answer is 'Light nuclei combining into a heavier nucleus'. The other options — 'Heavy nuclei splitting', 'Chemical bonding', and 'Electron capture' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Special Relativity
summary_sections.content_html:
Einstein's Two Postulates

  • 1. The laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames.
  • 2. The speed of light in vacuum (c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s) is the same for all observers, regardless of their motion.

Key Consequences

  • Time Dilation: Moving clocks run slow. t = γt₀, where γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²).
  • Length Contraction: Moving objects are shorter in the direction of motion. L = L₀/γ.
  • Mass-Energy Equivalence: E = mc². A small amount of mass corresponds to a huge amount of energy.

The Lorentz Factor (γ)

  • γ = 1 at v = 0 (no relativistic effects).
  • γ → ∞ as v → c (effects become extreme).
  • At everyday speeds, γ ≈ 1 and effects are negligible.

Relativistic Energy: E² = (pc)² + (mc²)². For a photon (m = 0): E = pc.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Einstein's second postulate of special relativity.

options.text: The speed of light is the same for all observers
Time runs faster for moving observers

quiz_questions.explanation: The speed of light in vacuum (c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s) is the same for all observers, regardless of their motion. The other options — 'Mass increases with speed', 'Time runs faster for moving observers', and 'Nothing can move' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is time dilation?

options.text: Moving clocks run slow: t = γt₀

quiz_questions.explanation: A dilation scales a figure by a factor k from a center point. If k > 1, it enlarges; if 0 < k < 1, it reduces. Dilations produce similar figures. The answer is Moving clocks run slow: t = γt₀. The other options — 'Moving clocks run fast', 'Time stops for all observers', and 'Time only exists at rest' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Lorentz factor (γ)?
quiz_questions.explanation: Factor the expression by finding common factors or using patterns (difference of squares, trinomial factoring, etc.). The factored form is γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²). The other options — 'γ = v/c', 'γ = mc²', and 'γ = 1 − v/c' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the mass-energy equivalence?
quiz_questions.explanation: Mass-Energy Equivalence : E = mc². The other options — 'E = mv²', 'E = mgh', and 'E = ½mv²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to γ as v → c?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Lorentz factor γ = 1/√(1 - v²/c²) describes relativistic effects. As an object's speed approaches c, γ → ∞, causing time dilation and length contraction. The answer is 'γ → infinity'. The other options — 'γ → 0', 'γ → 1', and 'γ → c' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is length contraction?

options.text: Moving objects are shorter in the direction of motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Length Contraction — Moving objects are shorter in the direction of motion. The other options — 'Moving objects are longer', 'Objects don't change length', and 'Length only changes at rest' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: At everyday speeds, γ is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: At everyday speeds, γ ≈ 1 and effects are negligible. The speed of light in vacuum (c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s) is the same for all observers, regardless of their motion. The other options — '0', 'Infinity', and 'c' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Quantum Mechanics
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Principles

  • Wave-Particle Duality: All matter exhibits both wave and particle properties. λ = h/p.
  • Quantization: Energy, angular momentum, and other properties come in discrete amounts.

The Wavefunction (ψ)

  • The wavefunction describes the quantum state of a particle.
  • |ψ|² gives the probability density of finding the particle at a given position.
  • The wavefunction evolves according to the Schrödinger equation.

Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle

  • It is impossible to simultaneously know both the exact position and momentum of a particle:
  • Δx · Δp ≥ ℏ/2 (where ℏ = h/2π).
  • This is not a measurement limitation — it's a fundamental property of nature.

Quantum Tunneling: A particle can pass through a potential barrier even if its energy is less than the barrier height — there is a nonzero probability of being found on the other side.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle?

options.text: Cannot simultaneously know exact position and momentum: Δx·Δp ≥ ℏ/2
Position and momentum can be known exactly

quiz_questions.explanation: Momentum (p = mv) is the product of mass and velocity. It is conserved in isolated systems. Greater mass or velocity means greater momentum. The answer is 'Cannot simultaneously know exact position and momentum: Δx·Δp ≥ ℏ/2'. The other options — 'Position and momentum can be known exactly', 'Only momentum is uncertain', and 'It applies only to photons' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does |ψ|² represent?

options.text: Probability density of finding the particle

quiz_questions.explanation: The absolute value |x| gives the distance from zero, always non-negative. For equations |expression| = k, split into two cases: expression = k and expression = -k. The answer is Probability density of finding the particle. The other options — 'Energy of the particle', 'Velocity of the particle', and 'Charge of the particle' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is quantum tunneling?

options.text: A particle passes through a barrier even when energy is less than the barrier
A particle bounces off all barriers
A particle gains energy from nothing

quiz_questions.explanation: Quantum mechanics describes matter and energy at the atomic scale. Photon energy E = hf. De Broglie wavelength λ = h/mv relates particle momentum to wave behavior. The answer is 'A particle passes through a barrier even when energy is less than the barrier'. The other options — 'A particle bounces off all barriers', 'A particle gains energy from nothing', and 'A particle loses all energy' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the de Broglie wavelength?
quiz_questions.explanation: Wave properties: v = fλ (velocity = frequency × wavelength). Amplitude determines energy/intensity. Frequency and wavelength are inversely related at constant wave speed. The answer is 'λ = h/p'. The other options — 'λ = p/h', 'λ = hv', and 'λ = mc' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is the uncertainty principle a measurement limitation?

options.text: No — it is a fundamental property of nature
Yes — better instruments would fix it

quiz_questions.explanation: This is not a measurement limitation — it's a fundamental property of nature. Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle It is impossible to simultaneously know both the exact position and momentum of a particle:. Δx · Δp ≥ ℏ/2 (where ℏ = h/2π). The other options — 'Yes — better instruments would fix it', 'Only for small particles', and 'It's just a theory' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What equation governs the wavefunction's evolution?
quiz_questions.explanation: The wavefunction evolves according to the Schrödinger equation. The other options — 'Newton's equation', 'Maxwell's equation', and 'Einstein's field equation' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why don't we observe quantum effects in everyday life?

options.text: Macroscopic objects have incredibly tiny de Broglie wavelengths

quiz_questions.explanation: Quantum mechanics describes matter and energy at the atomic scale. Photon energy E = hf. De Broglie wavelength λ = h/mv relates particle momentum to wave behavior. The answer is 'Macroscopic objects have incredibly tiny de Broglie wavelengths'. The other options — 'Quantum effects don't exist', 'Only atoms have wavefunctions', and 'Gravity cancels quantum effects' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Particle Physics & Standard Model

Fundamental Particles

  • Matter is made of quarks (make up protons/neutrons) and leptons (electrons, neutrinos).
  • There are 6 quarks: up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom.
  • There are 6 leptons: electron, muon, tau, and their corresponding neutrinos.

The Four Fundamental Forces

  • Strong Nuclear Force: Holds quarks together inside protons/neutrons. Mediated by gluons.
  • Electromagnetic Force: Between charged particles. Mediated by photons.
  • Weak Nuclear Force: Responsible for radioactive beta decay. Mediated by W and Z bosons.
  • Gravitational Force: Between masses. Hypothetically mediated by gravitons (unconfirmed).

The Higgs Boson

  • The Higgs field gives mass to fundamental particles. The Higgs boson was discovered in 2012 at CERN.

Antimatter: Every particle has an antiparticle (same mass, opposite charge). When they meet: annihilation → pure energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the two families of fundamental particles?
quiz_questions.explanation: Quarks are fundamental particles. Protons contain two up quarks and one down quark (uud); neutrons contain one up and two down (udd). The answer is 'Quarks and leptons'. The other options — 'Protons and neutrons', 'Electrons and photons', and 'Bosons and fermions only' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many types of quarks are there?
quiz_questions.explanation: Quarks are fundamental particles that combine to form hadrons (protons = uud, neutrons = udd). The Standard Model classifies all known fundamental particles and three of four forces. The answer is 'Six'. The other options — 'Three', 'Four', and 'Eight' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What mediates the electromagnetic force?
quiz_questions.explanation: A magnetic force acts on moving charges: F = qv × B. The direction follows the right-hand rule. Current-carrying wires create magnetic fields and experience forces in external fields. The answer is 'Photons'. The other options — 'Gluons', 'W and Z bosons', and 'Gravitons' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Higgs boson?

options.text: The particle giving mass to fundamental particles via the Higgs field

quiz_questions.explanation: The Higgs Boson The Higgs field gives mass to fundamental particles. The other options — 'The heaviest quark', 'A type of lepton', and 'The carrier of gravity' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: When was the Higgs boson discovered?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Higgs boson was discovered in 2012 at CERN. The Higgs Boson The Higgs field gives mass to fundamental particles. The other options — '2000, at Fermilab', '1998, at CERN', and '2020, at CERN' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is antimatter?

options.text: Particles with same mass but opposite charge

quiz_questions.explanation: Antimatter — Every particle has an antiparticle (same mass, opposite charge). The Higgs Boson The Higgs field gives mass to fundamental particles. The other options — 'Particles with no mass', 'Dark matter', and 'Particles moving backward in time' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens when a particle meets its antiparticle?
quiz_questions.explanation: When they meet — annihilation → pure energy. The other options — 'They pass through each other', 'They form a nucleus', and 'Nothing happens' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Distance, Displacement, Speed, Velocity
summary_sections.content_html:
Distance vs Displacement

  • Distance (scalar): Total path length traveled, regardless of direction. Always positive or zero.
  • Displacement (vector): Straight-line distance from start to finish, with direction. Can be positive, negative, or zero.

Speed vs Velocity

  • Speed (scalar): Rate of change of distance. Average speed = total distance / total time.
  • Velocity (vector): Rate of change of displacement. Average velocity = displacement / time.
  • Instantaneous velocity: Velocity at a specific instant, found as the limit of Δx/Δt as Δt → 0.

Key Relationships

  • An object moving in a circle at constant speed has changing velocity because direction changes.
  • Average speed ≥ |average velocity| (equality only for straight-line, one-direction motion).
  • On a position-time graph, velocity = slope of the curve.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between distance and displacement?

options.text: Distance is total path length (scalar); displacement is straight-line with direction (vector)
Distance has direction; displacement does not
Displacement is always larger than distance

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is the change in position — a vector quantity with both magnitude and direction. It differs from distance (scalar, total path length). Displacement = final position - initial position. The answer is 'Distance is total path length (scalar); displacement is straight-line with direction (vector)'. The other options — 'They are the same thing', 'Distance has direction; displacement does not', and 'Displacement is always larger than distance' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is velocity?
quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line distance from start to finish with direction (vector). It differs from distance, which is the total path length (scalar). The answer is 'Rate of change of displacement'. The other options — 'Rate of change of distance', 'Rate of change of acceleration', and 'Distance traveled per unit mass' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a position-time graph, what does the slope represent?
quiz_questions.explanation: Slope measures the rate of change: rise/run = (y₂ - y₁)/(x₂ - x₁). From the given information, the slope is Velocity. The other options — 'Acceleration', 'Distance', and 'Force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can average velocity equal zero for a moving object?

options.text: Yes — if it returns to its starting position
Only if it moves in a circle

quiz_questions.explanation: Yes — average velocity = displacement / time. If an object returns to its starting position, displacement = 0, so average velocity = 0, even though it was moving. The answer is 'Yes — if it returns to its starting position'. The other options — 'No, never', 'Only if it moves in a circle', and 'Only at very high speeds' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is instantaneous velocity?

options.text: Velocity at a specific instant in time

quiz_questions.explanation: Instantaneous velocity — Velocity at a specific instant, found as the limit of Δx/Δt as Δt → 0. Average velocity = displacement / time. The other options — 'The average of all velocities', 'The maximum velocity reached', and 'Velocity measured over one hour' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is average speed calculated?

options.text: Total distance divided by total time

quiz_questions.explanation: Average speed = total distance / total time. Distance vs Displacement Distance (scalar): Total path length traveled, regardless of direction. The other options — 'Displacement divided by time', 'Final speed minus initial speed', and 'Distance times time' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is it possible for speed to be constant while velocity changes?

options.text: Yes — for example, in circular motion
No, speed and velocity are the same
Only at the speed of light

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Relationships An object moving in a circle at constant speed has changing velocity because direction changes. Average speed ≥ |average velocity| (equality only for straight-line, one-direction motion). The other options — 'No, speed and velocity are the same', 'Only at the speed of light', and 'Only when acceleration is zero' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Acceleration
summary_sections.content_html:
What is Acceleration?

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time.

  • a = Δv / Δt = (v_f − v_i) / t
  • SI unit: m/s² (meters per second squared).
  • Acceleration is a vector — it has magnitude and direction.

Key Ideas

  • Positive acceleration: Velocity is increasing (speeding up in the positive direction, or slowing down in the negative direction).
  • Negative acceleration: Velocity is decreasing in the positive direction (deceleration).
  • An object can have zero velocity but nonzero acceleration (e.g., a ball at the top of its trajectory).
  • Uniform acceleration: Acceleration is constant — produces a straight line on a v-t graph.

Graphical Interpretation

  • On a velocity-time graph: acceleration = slope. Area under the curve = displacement.
  • On a position-time graph: acceleration causes the curve to bend (parabola for constant acceleration).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is acceleration?

options.text: The rate of change of velocity with respect to time
The rate of change of distance

quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity describing both speed and direction of motion. Unlike speed (scalar), velocity can be negative, indicating direction. The answer is 'The rate of change of velocity with respect to time'. The other options — 'The rate of change of distance', 'The speed of an object', and 'The total displacement' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of acceleration?
quiz_questions.explanation: SI unit — m/s² (meters per second squared). The other options — 'm/s', 'N', and 'kg·m/s' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the slope of a velocity-time graph represent?
quiz_questions.explanation: Slope measures the rate of change: rise/run = (y₂ - y₁)/(x₂ - x₁). From the given information, the slope is Acceleration. The other options — 'Velocity', 'Displacement', and 'Force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the area under a velocity-time graph represent?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area under a curve y = f(x) from a to b is found by integration: ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx. For simple shapes (triangles, trapezoids), use geometry formulas. The answer is Displacement. The other options — 'Acceleration', 'Speed', and 'Force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can an object have zero velocity but nonzero acceleration?

options.text: Yes — like a ball at the top of its trajectory

quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity = displacement/time (vector). Speed = distance/time (scalar). For constant acceleration: v = v₀ + at, and average velocity = (v + v₀)/2. The answer is 'Yes — like a ball at the top of its trajectory'. The other options — 'No, that is impossible', 'Only in outer space', and 'Only for very heavy objects' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you double the net force on an object (constant mass), acceleration:
quiz_questions.explanation: A free body diagram shows all forces acting on an object. The net force (vector sum) determines acceleration via F_net = ma. If F_net = 0, the object is in equilibrium. The answer is 'Doubles'. The other options — 'Halves', 'Stays the same', and 'Quadruples' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is acceleration a scalar or a vector?
quiz_questions.explanation: Vectors have both magnitude and direction (velocity, force, acceleration). Scalars have only magnitude (speed, mass, energy). Vectors can be decomposed into perpendicular components. The answer is 'Vector'. The other options — 'Scalar', 'Both', and 'Neither' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Graphical Analysis of Motion
summary_sections.content_html:
Position-Time (x-t) Graphs

  • Slope = velocity. A steeper slope means faster motion.
  • Straight line = constant velocity. Curved line = changing velocity (acceleration).
  • Horizontal line = object at rest.

Velocity-Time (v-t) Graphs

  • Slope = acceleration. Positive slope = speeding up; negative slope = slowing down.
  • Area under the curve = displacement. Area above the time axis is positive; below is negative.
  • Horizontal line = constant velocity (zero acceleration).

Acceleration-Time (a-t) Graphs

  • Area under the curve = change in velocity (Δv).
  • Horizontal line = constant (uniform) acceleration.

Converting Between Graphs

  • Differentiate x-t → v-t → a-t (take slopes).
  • Integrate a-t → v-t → x-t (take areas).

quiz_questions.question_text: On a position-time graph, what does a horizontal line mean?
quiz_questions.explanation: Horizontal line = object at rest. The other options — 'The object has constant velocity', 'The object is accelerating', and 'The object is decelerating' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a v-t graph, what does a horizontal line mean?
quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity describing both speed and direction of motion. Unlike speed (scalar), velocity can be negative, indicating direction. The answer is 'Constant velocity (zero acceleration)'. The other options — 'The object is at rest', 'Constant acceleration', and 'The object is decelerating' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What shape does constant acceleration produce on a position-time graph?
quiz_questions.explanation: The kinematic equations for constant acceleration: v = v₀ + at, x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at², v² = v₀² + 2a(x - x₀). The answer is 'A parabola'. The other options — 'A straight line', 'A horizontal line', and 'A circle' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you go from an x-t graph to a v-t graph?
quiz_questions.explanation: Converting Between Graphs Differentiate x-t → v-t → a-t (take slopes). Integrate a-t → v-t → x-t (take areas). The other options — 'Take the area (integrate)', 'Multiply by time', and 'Add a constant' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: On an a-t graph, what does the area under the curve represent?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area under a curve y = f(x) from a to b is found by integration: ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx. For simple shapes (triangles, trapezoids), use geometry formulas. The answer is Change in velocity (Δv). The other options — 'Displacement', 'Acceleration', and 'Force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a position-time graph, what does a curved line mean?
quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity describing both speed and direction of motion. Unlike speed (scalar), velocity can be negative, indicating direction. The answer is 'Changing velocity (acceleration)'. The other options — 'Constant velocity', 'The object is at rest', and 'The object is moving backward' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you go from a v-t graph to an x-t graph?
quiz_questions.explanation: Integrate a-t → v-t → x-t (take areas). Converting Between Graphs Differentiate x-t → v-t → a-t (take slopes). The other options — 'Take the slope', 'Divide by time', and 'Square the values' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Equations of Motion (Constant Acceleration)
summary_sections.content_html:
The Kinematic Equations (SUVAT)

These equations apply when acceleration a is constant. The five variables are: s (displacement), u (initial velocity), v (final velocity), a (acceleration), t (time).

  • v = u + at — final velocity from initial velocity and acceleration.
  • s = ut + ½at² — displacement from initial velocity, acceleration, and time.
  • v² = u² + 2as — relates velocities to displacement (no time needed).
  • s = ½(u + v)t — displacement from average velocity.

Using the Equations

  • Identify what you know (3 of 5 variables) and what you want to find.
  • Choose the equation that contains your known variables and the unknown.
  • Be consistent with signs: pick a positive direction and stick with it.

Important Notes

  • These equations assume constant acceleration — they fail if acceleration changes.
  • For free fall, use a = g = 9.8 m/s² (downward).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which equation is v = u + at?

options.text: A kinematic (SUVAT) equation for constant acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity: a = Δv/Δt. It's a vector — positive means speeding up (or decelerating in the negative direction). The answer is 'A kinematic (SUVAT) equation for constant acceleration'. The other options — 'Newton's Second Law', 'The work-energy theorem', and 'The impulse-momentum theorem' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: When do the kinematic equations apply?
quiz_questions.explanation: The SUVAT (kinematic) equations relate displacement (s), initial velocity (u), final velocity (v), acceleration (a), and time (t) for constant acceleration: v = u + at, s = ut + ½at², v² = u² + 2as. The answer is 'Only when acceleration is constant'. The other options — 'Always', 'Only in free fall', and 'Only for objects at rest' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many SUVAT knowns do you need to solve a problem?
quiz_questions.explanation: The SUVAT (kinematic) equations relate displacement (s), initial velocity (u), final velocity (v), acceleration (a), and time (t) for constant acceleration: v = u + at, s = ut + ½at², v² = u² + 2as. The answer is 'Three'. The other options — 'Two', 'Four', and 'Five' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car starts from rest. What is u?
quiz_questions.explanation: 'Starts from rest' means the initial velocity u = 0 m/s. Use this in kinematic equations like v = u + at and s = ut + ½at². The answer is '0 m/s'. The other options — '9.8 m/s', 'Unknown', and 'Equal to v' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula s = ut + ½at²  used for?

options.text: Finding displacement given initial velocity, acceleration, and time

quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity describing both speed and direction of motion. Unlike speed (scalar), velocity can be negative, indicating direction. The answer is 'Finding displacement given initial velocity, acceleration, and time'. The other options — 'Finding force', 'Finding energy', and 'Finding momentum' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does v² = u² + 2as allow you to find without knowing?
quiz_questions.explanation: The five variables are: s (displacement), u (initial velocity), v (final velocity), a (acceleration), t (time). v = u + at — final velocity from initial velocity and acceleration. s = ut + ½at² — displacement from initial velocity, acceleration, and time. v² = u² + 2as — relates velocities to displacement (no time needed).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the acceleration due to gravity (g)?
quiz_questions.explanation: In free fall near Earth's surface, all objects accelerate at g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (ignoring air resistance). This value comes from Newton's law of gravitation: g = GM/R². The answer is '9.8 m/s² downward'. The other options — '9.8 m/s upward', '10 N/kg', and '3 × 10⁸ m/s²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Free Fall & Projectile Motion

Free Fall

  • Free fall is motion under gravity alone, with no air resistance.
  • All objects in free fall accelerate at g ≈ 9.8 m/s² downward, regardless of mass.
  • Use kinematic equations with a = −g (taking upward as positive).

Projectile Motion

  • A projectile moves in two dimensions: horizontal (constant velocity) and vertical (free fall).
  • Horizontal: x = v₀ₓ · t (no acceleration).
  • Vertical: y = v₀ᵧ · t − ½gt² (acceleration = −g).
  • The path traces a parabola.

Key Formulas

  • Range (horizontal distance): R = (v₀² sin 2θ) / g.
  • Maximum height: H = (v₀ sin θ)² / (2g).
  • Time of flight: T = 2v₀ sin θ / g.
  • Maximum range occurs at θ = 45°.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is free fall?

options.text: Motion under gravity alone with no air resistance

quiz_questions.explanation: In free fall, all objects accelerate at g = 9.8 m/s² regardless of mass (ignoring air resistance). Use kinematics with a = g. The answer is 'Motion under gravity alone with no air resistance'. The other options — 'Falling with a parachute', 'Any downward motion', and 'Motion at terminal velocity' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In projectile motion, horizontal acceleration is:
quiz_questions.explanation: In projectile motion (ignoring air resistance): horizontal velocity stays constant (a_x = 0), vertical acceleration = g = 9.8 m/s² downward. Range is maximized at 45°. Use kinematics equations for each component independently. The answer is 'Zero'. The other options — '9.8 m/s²', 'Equal to vertical acceleration', and 'Depends on launch angle' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: At what launch angle is the range of a projectile maximized?
quiz_questions.explanation: In projectile motion (ignoring air resistance): horizontal velocity stays constant (a_x = 0), vertical acceleration = g = 9.8 m/s² downward. Range is maximized at 45°. Use kinematics equations for each component independently. The answer is '45°'. The other options — '30°', '60°', and '90°' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What shape does a projectile's path trace?
quiz_questions.explanation: In projectile motion (ignoring air resistance): horizontal velocity stays constant (a_x = 0), vertical acceleration = g = 9.8 m/s² downward. Range is maximized at 45°. Use kinematics equations for each component independently. The answer is 'A parabola'. The other options — 'A straight line', 'A circle', and 'An ellipse' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: At the top of a projectile's path, what is the vertical velocity?
quiz_questions.explanation: In projectile motion (ignoring air resistance): horizontal velocity stays constant (a_x = 0), vertical acceleration = g = 9.8 m/s² downward. Range is maximized at 45°. Use kinematics equations for each component independently. The answer is 'Zero'. The other options — 'Maximum', 'Equal to horizontal velocity', and '9.8 m/s' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Do heavier objects fall faster in free fall (no air resistance)?

options.text: No — all objects accelerate at g

quiz_questions.explanation: In free fall, all objects accelerate at g = 9.8 m/s² regardless of mass (ignoring air resistance). Use kinematics with a = g. The answer is 'No — all objects accelerate at g'. The other options — 'Yes, heavier objects fall faster', 'Only near Earth's surface', and 'It depends on their shape' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for time of flight of a projectile?
quiz_questions.explanation: In projectile motion (ignoring air resistance): horizontal velocity stays constant (a_x = 0), vertical acceleration = g = 9.8 m/s² downward. Range is maximized at 45°. Use kinematics equations for each component independently. The answer is 'T = 2v₀ sin θ / g'. The other options — 'T = v₀ / g', 'T = v₀ cos θ / g', and 'T = 2v₀ / g' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Relative Motion & Reference Frames

Reference Frames

  • A reference frame is a coordinate system used to measure position and motion. All motion is relative to a chosen frame.
  • Inertial frame: A frame not accelerating (Newton's laws hold directly).
  • Non-inertial frame: An accelerating frame where fictitious forces appear.

Relative Velocity

  • The velocity of object A relative to object B: v_AB = v_A − v_B.
  • If two cars move in the same direction at 60 and 40 km/h, relative velocity = 20 km/h.
  • If moving toward each other, relative velocity = sum of speeds.

Applications

  • River crossing: A boat's velocity relative to ground = boat velocity relative to water + water velocity relative to ground.
  • Rain problems: The apparent direction of rain changes when you move.
  • The Galilean velocity addition formula applies at speeds much less than the speed of light.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a reference frame?

options.text: A coordinate system used to measure position and motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Relative Motion & Reference Frames Reference Frames A reference frame is a coordinate system used to measure position and motion. All motion is relative to a chosen frame. The other options — 'A type of laboratory equipment', 'A fixed point in space', and 'A unit of measurement' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you find the velocity of A relative to B?
quiz_questions.explanation: Relative Velocity The velocity of object A relative to object B: v_AB = v_A − v_B. If two cars move in the same direction at 60 and 40 km/h, relative velocity = 20 km/h. The other options — 'v_AB = v_A + v_B', 'v_AB = v_A × v_B', and 'v_AB = v_A / v_B' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two cars moving at 60 and 40 km/h in the same direction: relative velocity?
quiz_questions.explanation: If two cars move in the same direction at 60 and 40 km/h, relative velocity = 20 km/h. If moving toward each other, relative velocity = sum of speeds. The other options — '100 km/h', '50 km/h', and '2400 km/h' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

options.text: A non-accelerating frame where Newton's laws apply directly

quiz_questions.question_text: When does Galilean relativity break down?

options.text: At speeds close to the speed of light

quiz_questions.explanation: The Galilean velocity addition formula applies at speeds much less than the speed of light. The other options — 'At very low speeds', 'Only in water', and 'Never' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does rain appear angled when you run?

options.text: Your motion changes the rain's velocity relative to you

quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity describing both speed and direction of motion. Unlike speed (scalar), velocity can be negative, indicating direction. The answer is 'Your motion changes the rain's velocity relative to you'. The other options — 'The wind always blows horizontally', 'Rain never falls straight', and 'It's an optical illusion' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a river crossing, the boat's ground velocity is found by:

options.text: Vector sum of boat velocity relative to water and water velocity relative to ground
Dividing boat speed by current speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Applications River crossing — A boat's velocity relative to ground = boat velocity relative to water + water velocity relative to ground. The other options — 'Subtracting all velocities', 'Using only the boat's speed', and 'Dividing boat speed by current speed' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Force & Interaction

What is Force?

  • A force is a push or pull that can change an object's velocity (speed and/or direction).
  • Force is a vector quantity — it has magnitude and direction. SI unit: Newton (N).
  • 1 N = 1 kg · m/s² — the force needed to accelerate 1 kg by 1 m/s².

Types of Forces

  • Contact forces: Require physical touch — friction, tension, normal force, applied force, air resistance.
  • Non-contact (field) forces: Act at a distance — gravity, electromagnetic force, nuclear forces.

Free-Body Diagrams

  • A free-body diagram shows all forces acting on a single object as arrows from its center of mass.
  • Arrow length represents magnitude; direction represents the direction of the force.
  • The net force (ΣF) is the vector sum of all forces. If ΣF = 0, the object is in equilibrium (no acceleration).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of force?
quiz_questions.explanation: SI units: Force = Newton (N = kg·m/s²), Energy/Work = Joule (J = kg·m²/s²), Power = Watt (W = J/s), Momentum = kg·m/s. 1 horsepower ≈ 746 W. The answer is 'Newton (N)'. The other options — 'Joule (J)', 'Watt (W)', and 'Pascal (Pa)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a free-body diagram?

options.text: A diagram showing all forces on a single object as arrows
A diagram of an object in free fall
A graph of force vs. time
A picture of an object with no forces

quiz_questions.explanation: Free-Body Diagrams A free-body diagram shows all forces acting on a single object as arrows from its center of mass. The other options — 'A diagram of an object in free fall', 'A graph of force vs. time', and 'A picture of an object with no forces' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the net force?

options.text: The vector sum of all forces acting on an object
The largest force acting on an object
Any single force on the object

quiz_questions.explanation: A free body diagram shows all forces acting on an object. The net force (vector sum) determines acceleration via F_net = ma. If F_net = 0, the object is in equilibrium. The answer is 'The vector sum of all forces acting on an object'. The other options — 'The largest force acting on an object', 'The force of gravity', and 'Any single force on the object' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens when the net force on an object is zero?

options.text: The object is in equilibrium — no acceleration
The object must be at rest

quiz_questions.explanation: A free body diagram shows all forces acting on an object. The net force (vector sum) determines acceleration via F_net = ma. If F_net = 0, the object is in equilibrium. The answer is 'The object is in equilibrium — no acceleration'. The other options — 'The object must be at rest', 'The object must be moving', and 'The object accelerates' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is force a scalar or vector?
quiz_questions.explanation: Vectors have both magnitude and direction (velocity, force, acceleration). Scalars have only magnitude (speed, mass, energy). Vectors can be decomposed into perpendicular components. The answer is 'Vector'. The other options — 'Scalar', 'Both', and 'Neither' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a contact force?

options.text: A force acting at a distance

quiz_questions.explanation: Types of Forces Contact forces : Require physical touch — friction, tension, normal force, applied force, air resistance. The other options — 'A force acting at a distance', 'A force from gravity', and 'An electromagnetic force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 1 Newton in base units?
quiz_questions.explanation: Derived units are combinations of base units. Newton (N) = kg·m/s² (force). Joule (J) = kg·m²/s² (energy). Watt (W) = J/s (power). Pascal (Pa) = N/m² (pressure). The answer is '1 kg·m/s²'. The other options — '1 kg·m/s', '1 kg·m²/s²', and '1 kg/m·s²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Newton’s First Law

Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia)

An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and direction, unless acted upon by a net external force.

  • Inertia: The resistance of an object to changes in its state of motion. More mass = more inertia.
  • If the net force on an object is zero, its velocity does not change (it either stays still or moves at constant velocity).

Equilibrium

  • Static equilibrium: Object is at rest and ΣF = 0.
  • Dynamic equilibrium: Object moves at constant velocity and ΣF = 0.

Everyday Examples

  • A passenger lurches forward when a bus brakes suddenly — their body's inertia keeps them moving forward.
  • A hockey puck slides on ice (very low friction) and barely slows down.
  • Objects in deep space, far from gravitational influences, travel in straight lines at constant speeds indefinitely.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is Newton's First Law also called?
quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's First Law (inertia): An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity, unless acted upon by a net external force. The answer is 'The Law of Inertia'. The other options — 'The Law of Acceleration', 'The Law of Action-Reaction', and 'The Law of Gravity' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is inertia?

options.text: The resistance of an object to changes in its state of motion
The force needed to move an object

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia — The resistance of an object to changes in its state of motion. Key Concepts — Newton’s First Law Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and direction , unless acted upon by a net external force.

quiz_questions.question_text: If no net force acts on a moving object, it will:
quiz_questions.explanation: A free body diagram shows all forces acting on an object. The net force (vector sum) determines acceleration via F_net = ma. If F_net = 0, the object is in equilibrium. The answer is 'Continue at constant velocity'. The other options — 'Slow down and stop', 'Speed up', and 'Change direction' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is static equilibrium?

options.text: An object at rest with ΣF = 0
An object moving at constant speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Equilibrium Static equilibrium — Object is at rest and ΣF = 0. Key Concepts — Newton’s First Law Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and direction , unless acted upon by a net external force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bus brakes suddenly. Why do passengers lurch forward?

options.text: Their bodies' inertia keeps them moving forward

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. An object at rest stays at rest, and a moving object keeps moving, unless acted on by a net force (Newton's First Law). The answer is 'Their bodies' inertia keeps them moving forward'. The other options — 'The bus pushes them forward', 'Gravity pulls them forward', and 'Air resistance pushes them' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What determines how much inertia an object has?
quiz_questions.explanation: More mass = more inertia. Key Concepts — Newton’s First Law Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and direction , unless acted upon by a net external force. The other options — 'Its speed', 'Its color', and 'Its temperature' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can an object be in equilibrium while moving?

options.text: Yes — this is called dynamic equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: An object is in mechanical equilibrium when the net force is zero (ΣF = 0). For static equilibrium, it's also at rest. For translational equilibrium, velocity is constant. The answer is 'Yes — this is called dynamic equilibrium'. The other options — 'No, equilibrium requires rest', 'Only in space', and 'Only at very low speeds' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Newton’s Second Law (F = ma)

Newton's Second Law

The net force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration:

ΣF = ma

  • Greater force → greater acceleration (for the same mass).
  • Greater mass → less acceleration (for the same force).
  • Acceleration is always in the same direction as the net force.

Weight vs Mass

  • Mass (kg): Amount of matter; does not change with location.
  • Weight (N): The gravitational force on an object. W = mg, where g ≈ 9.8 m/s² on Earth.
  • A 70 kg person weighs about 686 N on Earth but only ~114 N on the Moon.

Problem-Solving

  • Draw a free-body diagram.
  • Choose a coordinate system.
  • Write ΣF = ma for each axis.
  • Solve for the unknown.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Newton's Second Law.
quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma. The net force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration. The answer is 'ΣF = ma'. The other options — 'F = mv', 'F = mgh', and 'F = kx' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you double the net force on an object (mass constant), acceleration:
quiz_questions.explanation: A free body diagram shows all forces acting on an object. The net force (vector sum) determines acceleration via F_net = ma. If F_net = 0, the object is in equilibrium. The answer is 'Doubles'. The other options — 'Halves', 'Stays the same', and 'Quadruples' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the weight of a 70 kg person on Earth?
quiz_questions.explanation: A 70 kg person weighs about 686 N on Earth but only ~114 N on the Moon. The other options — '70 N', '700 kg', and '7 N' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between mass and weight?

options.text: Mass is amount of matter (kg); weight is gravitational force (N)

quiz_questions.explanation: Weight vs Mass Mass (kg): Amount of matter; does not change with location. Weight (N): The gravitational force on an object. The other options — 'They are the same thing', 'Mass is measured in Newtons', and 'Weight is measured in kilograms' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does mass change with location?
quiz_questions.explanation: Cations are positive ions (formed by losing electrons); anions are negative ions (formed by gaining electrons). Metals typically form cations; nonmetals form anions. The answer is 'No — mass is constant'. The other options — 'Yes — mass changes with gravity', 'Only on the Moon', and 'Only in space' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5 kg object accelerates at 3 m/s². What is the net force?
quiz_questions.explanation: A free body diagram shows all forces acting on an object. The net force (vector sum) determines acceleration via F_net = ma. If F_net = 0, the object is in equilibrium. The answer is '15 N'. The other options — '8 N', '1.67 N', and '150 N' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Acceleration is always in which direction relative to net force?
quiz_questions.explanation: A free body diagram shows all forces acting on an object. The net force (vector sum) determines acceleration via F_net = ma. If F_net = 0, the object is in equilibrium. The answer is 'The same direction'. The other options — 'The opposite direction', 'Perpendicular', and 'It varies' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Newton’s Third Law

Newton's Third Law

For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.

  • If object A exerts a force on object B, then B exerts a force on A that is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
  • Action-reaction pairs act on different objects — they never cancel each other.

Examples

  • You push a wall → the wall pushes you back with equal force.
  • Earth pulls you down (gravity) → you pull Earth up with the same force (but Earth barely moves due to its huge mass).
  • A rocket expels exhaust gases downward → the gases push the rocket upward.

Common Misconceptions

  • Action-reaction pairs do NOT cancel because they act on different objects.
  • The "heavier" object accelerates less because a = F/m (same F, bigger m).
  • Newton's third law always applies — there are no exceptions.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Newton's Third Law.

options.text: For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction
Objects at rest stay at rest

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law: For every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force. The answer is 'For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction'. The other options — 'Objects at rest stay at rest', 'F = ma', and 'Energy is conserved' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Action-reaction pairs act on how many objects?
quiz_questions.explanation: Action-reaction pairs act on different objects — they never cancel each other. Common Misconceptions Action-reaction pairs do NOT cancel because they act on different objects. The other options — 'The same object', 'Three objects', and 'It depends on the situation' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why don't action-reaction pairs cancel each other?

options.text: Because they act on different objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Common Misconceptions Action-reaction pairs do NOT cancel because they act on different objects. The other options — 'Because one is always larger', 'They do cancel', and 'Because of friction' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a truck hits a small car, which experiences the greater force?
quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law: when two objects interact, they exert equal and opposite forces on each other. The truck and car experience the SAME force, but different accelerations (F = ma). The answer is 'Both experience the same force'. The other options — 'The truck', 'The car', and 'Neither — no force occurs' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does a rocket propel itself in space?

options.text: It expels exhaust gases backward; the gases push the rocket forward

quiz_questions.explanation: A rocket expels exhaust gases downward → the gases push the rocket upward. The other options — 'It pushes against the air', 'It uses magnetic fields', and 'Solar wind pushes it' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does Newton's Third Law have exceptions?
quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law: For every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force. The answer is 'No — it always applies'. The other options — 'Yes, not in space', 'Yes, not for gravity', and 'Yes, not at high speeds' — are all valid examples, making 'No — it always applies' the correct exception.

quiz_questions.question_text: You push a wall. What is the reaction force?

options.text: The wall pushes back on you with equal and opposite force

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law: when two objects interact, they exert equal and opposite forces on each other. The truck and car experience the SAME force, but different accelerations (F = ma). The answer is 'The wall pushes back on you with equal and opposite force'. The other options — 'Gravity pulls you down', 'Friction pushes you sideways', and 'Nothing — walls cannot exert force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Friction & Tension

Friction

  • Static friction (fₛ): Prevents an object from starting to move. fₛ ≤ μₛN (up to a maximum).
  • Kinetic friction (fₖ): Acts on a sliding object. fₖ = μₖN (constant).
  • μₛ > μₖ — it takes more force to start motion than to maintain it.
  • N = normal force; μ = coefficient of friction (dimensionless, depends on surfaces).

Tension

  • Tension is the pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable.
  • In an ideal (massless, inextensible) string, tension is the same throughout.
  • A pulley changes the direction of tension but not its magnitude (ideal pulley).

Solving Problems

  • Draw a free-body diagram for each object.
  • Apply ΣF = ma to each object separately.
  • For connected objects, they share the same acceleration (if the string is taut).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is larger: static or kinetic friction coefficient?
quiz_questions.explanation: Static friction prevents motion: f_s ≤ μ_s·N. It adjusts up to its maximum value (μ_s·N). Static friction coefficient is always ≥ kinetic. The answer is 'Static friction coefficient (μₛ)'. The other options — 'Kinetic friction coefficient (μₖ)', 'They are always equal', and 'It depends on the surface' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the normal force?

options.text: The perpendicular contact force a surface exerts on an object

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is the contact force perpendicular to a surface. On a flat surface: N = mg. On an incline: N = mg·cos(θ). It adjusts to prevent objects from passing through surfaces. The answer is 'The perpendicular contact force a surface exerts on an object'. The other options — 'The force of gravity', 'The friction force', and 'The applied force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is tension?

options.text: The pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable

quiz_questions.explanation: Tension Tension is the pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable. In an ideal (massless, inextensible) string, tension is the same throughout. The other options — 'A pushing force', 'The force of gravity', and 'Air resistance' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If N = 50 N and μₖ = 0.3, what is the kinetic friction force?
quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic friction opposes motion: f_k = μ_k·N, where μ_k is the kinetic friction coefficient and N is the normal force. The answer is '15 N'. The other options — '50 N', '150 N', and '0.006 N' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does it take more force to start an object moving than to keep it moving?

options.text: Static friction (max) is greater than kinetic friction

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic friction (fₖ) : Acts on a sliding object. fₖ = μₖN (constant). μₛ > μₖ — it takes more force to start motion than to maintain it. The other options — 'Gravity increases at rest', 'Kinetic friction is larger', and 'Objects gain mass at rest' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an ideal pulley, what happens to tension?

options.text: It changes direction but not magnitude

quiz_questions.explanation: A pulley changes the direction of tension but not its magnitude (ideal pulley). In an ideal (massless, inextensible) string, tension is the same throughout. The other options — 'It doubles', 'It halves', and 'It becomes zero' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: For connected objects with a taut string, what do they share?
quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity: a = Δv/Δt. It's a vector — positive means speeding up (or decelerating in the negative direction). The answer is 'The same acceleration'. The other options — 'The same mass', 'The same velocity always', and 'The same force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Normal Force & Inclined Planes

Normal Force

  • The normal force (N) is the perpendicular contact force a surface exerts on an object.
  • On a flat surface: N = mg (weight). On an incline: N = mg cos θ.
  • The normal force adjusts to prevent objects from passing through surfaces.

Inclined Planes

  • Decompose weight into two components: parallel (mg sin θ, down the slope) and perpendicular (mg cos θ, into the surface).
  • Without friction, acceleration down the slope = g sin θ.
  • With friction: a = g sin θ − μₖg cos θ (down the slope).

Key Angles

  • The angle of repose is the steepest angle at which an object stays stationary: tan θ = μₛ.
  • At steeper angles, the object slides; at shallower angles, static friction holds it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the component of weight parallel to an incline?
quiz_questions.explanation: Inclined Planes Decompose weight into two components: parallel (mg sin θ, down the slope) and perpendicular (mg cos θ, into the surface). The other options — 'mg cos θ', 'mg tan θ', and 'mg/sin θ' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the normal force on an inclined plane?
quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is the contact force perpendicular to a surface. On a flat surface: N = mg. On an incline: N = mg·cos(θ). It adjusts to prevent objects from passing through surfaces. The answer is 'N = mg cos θ'. The other options — 'N = mg', 'N = mg sin θ', and 'N = mg tan θ' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a frictionless incline, what is the acceleration?
quiz_questions.explanation: Friction opposes relative motion between surfaces. Static friction (f_s ≤ μ_s·N) prevents motion; kinetic friction (f_k = μ_k·N) opposes motion. The answer is 'g sin θ'. The other options — 'g cos θ', 'g', and 'g tan θ' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the angle of repose?

options.text: The steepest angle at which an object stays stationary
The angle of a ramp in a lab
The angle at which friction disappears

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Angles The angle of repose is the steepest angle at which an object stays stationary: tan θ = μₛ. The other options — 'The angle of a ramp in a lab', '45 degrees', and 'The angle at which friction disappears' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does the normal force always equal mg?

options.text: No — only on flat horizontal surfaces

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is the contact force perpendicular to a surface. On a flat surface: N = mg. On an incline: N = mg·cos(θ). It adjusts to prevent objects from passing through surfaces. The answer is 'No — only on flat horizontal surfaces'. The other options — 'Yes, always', 'Only on inclines', and 'Only in free fall' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do we decompose forces on an inclined plane?

options.text: To separate motion along the slope from motion perpendicular to it

quiz_questions.explanation: Inclined Planes Decompose weight into two components: parallel (mg sin θ, down the slope) and perpendicular (mg cos θ, into the surface). Key Concepts — Normal Force & Inclined Planes Normal Force The normal force (N) is the perpendicular contact force a surface exerts on an object.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a steeper incline, the component of gravity along the slope:
quiz_questions.explanation: Slope measures the rate of change: rise/run = (y₂ - y₁)/(x₂ - x₁). From the given information, the slope is Increases. The other options — 'Decreases', 'Stays the same', and 'Becomes zero' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Circular Motion & Centripetal Force

Uniform Circular Motion

  • An object moving in a circle at constant speed is always accelerating because its direction changes.
  • The acceleration points toward the center of the circle — called centripetal acceleration.
  • a_c = v²/r, where v is speed and r is radius.

Centripetal Force

  • The net inward force causing circular motion: F_c = mv²/r = mω²r.
  • Centripetal force is not a new type of force — it's provided by existing forces (tension, gravity, friction, normal force).

Examples

  • A car turning on a road: friction provides centripetal force.
  • A ball on a string: tension provides centripetal force.
  • The Moon orbiting Earth: gravity provides centripetal force.

Period and Frequency: T = 2πr/v, f = 1/T, ω = 2πf.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is centripetal acceleration?

options.text: Acceleration directed toward the center of circular motion: a_c = v²/r

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration points toward the center of circular motion: a_c = v²/r. The centripetal force F_c = mv²/r is provided by gravity, tension, friction, or other forces. The answer is 'Acceleration directed toward the center of circular motion: a_c = v²/r'. The other options — 'Acceleration away from the center', 'Acceleration along the tangent', and 'Acceleration due to gravity' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is centripetal force a new type of force?

options.text: No — it is provided by existing forces
Yes — it only appears in circular motion
Yes — it is a fundamental force
