quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration points toward the center of circular motion: a_c = v²/r. The centripetal force F_c = mv²/r is provided by gravity, tension, friction, or other forces. The answer is 'No — it is provided by existing forces'. The other options — 'Yes — it only appears in circular motion', 'Yes — it is a fundamental force', and 'Yes — it is a quantum effect' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What provides centripetal force for a car turning on a road?
quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration points toward the center of circular motion: a_c = v²/r. The centripetal force F_c = mv²/r is provided by gravity, tension, friction, or other forces. The answer is 'Friction between tires and road'. The other options — 'Gravity', 'The engine', and 'Air resistance' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In uniform circular motion, is speed constant?

options.text: Yes, and velocity is also constant

quiz_questions.explanation: For uniform circular motion, the object moves at constant speed in a circle. Centripetal acceleration a_c = v²/r points toward the center. The centripetal force provides this acceleration. The answer is 'Yes, but velocity changes direction'. The other options — 'No, speed varies', 'Yes, and velocity is also constant', and 'Speed is zero' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for period of circular motion?
quiz_questions.explanation: For uniform circular motion, the object moves at constant speed in a circle. Centripetal acceleration a_c = v²/r points toward the center. The centripetal force provides this acceleration. The answer is 'T = 2πr/v'. The other options — 'T = πr²/v', 'T = v/(2πr)', and 'T = 2πv/r' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the relationship between period and frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: Period (T) and frequency (f) are reciprocals: T = 1/f and f = 1/T. If T = 0.5 s, then f = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz. The answer is 'f = 1/T'. The other options — 'f = T', 'f = 2T', and 'f = T²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Write centripetal force in terms of angular velocity.
quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration points toward the center of circular motion: a_c = v²/r. The centripetal force F_c = mv²/r is provided by gravity, tension, friction, or other forces. The answer is 'F_c = mω²r'. The other options — 'F_c = mωr', 'F_c = mω²/r', and 'F_c = mω/r²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Non-Inertial Frames & Pseudo-Forces

Non-Inertial Reference Frames

  • A non-inertial frame is one that is accelerating. Newton's laws don't directly apply without modifications.
  • To use F = ma in a non-inertial frame, we introduce pseudo-forces (fictitious forces).

Common Pseudo-Forces

  • Pseudo-force in a linearly accelerating frame: F_pseudo = −ma_frame. You feel pushed backward when a car accelerates forward.
  • Centrifugal force: An outward pseudo-force felt in a rotating frame. F = mω²r (outward). It's not a real force — it's the result of inertia in a rotating reference frame.
  • Coriolis force: Affects moving objects in a rotating frame. It deflects winds on Earth (right in the Northern Hemisphere, left in the Southern).

Key Point

  • Pseudo-forces are mathematical tools — they have no real physical source (no action-reaction pair).
  • In an inertial frame, the same phenomena are explained by the absence of centripetal force, not by centrifugal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a pseudo-force?

options.text: A fictitious force added in a non-inertial frame to make F = ma work

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. An object at rest stays at rest, and a moving object keeps moving, unless acted on by a net force (Newton's First Law). The answer is 'A fictitious force added in a non-inertial frame to make F = ma work'. The other options — 'A real contact force', 'A gravitational force', and 'An electromagnetic force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

options.text: No — it is a fictitious force in a rotating frame
Yes — it is a fundamental force

The other options — 'Yes — it is a fundamental force', 'Yes — it is a contact force', and 'Yes — it acts in all frames' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The other options — 'To the left', 'Downward', and 'Upward' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Work & Energy Transfer

Work

  • Work is done when a force causes displacement: W = Fd cos θ, where θ is the angle between the force and displacement.
  • SI unit: Joule (J). 1 J = 1 N·m.
  • Work is positive if force and displacement are in the same direction, negative if opposite, and zero if perpendicular (θ = 90°).

Energy Transfer

  • Work is the mechanism for transferring energy between objects or systems.
  • Positive work → energy is added to the object (it speeds up).
  • Negative work → energy is removed from the object (it slows down).

Examples

  • Pushing a box across the floor: you do positive work; friction does negative work.
  • Carrying a box horizontally at constant height: gravity does zero work (force perpendicular to displacement).
  • Lifting a box: you do positive work against gravity; gravity does negative work on the box.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for work?
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Work & Energy Transfer Work Work is done when a force causes displacement: W = Fd cos θ , where θ is the angle between the force and displacement. The other options — 'W = Fd sin θ', 'W = mv²', and 'W = mgh' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of work?
quiz_questions.explanation: SI units: Force = Newton (N = kg·m/s²), Energy/Work = Joule (J = kg·m²/s²), Power = Watt (W = J/s), Momentum = kg·m/s. 1 horsepower ≈ 746 W. The answer is 'Joule (J)'. The other options — 'Watt (W)', 'Newton (N)', and 'Pascal (Pa)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: When is work zero?

options.text: When force is perpendicular to displacement

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line distance from start to finish with direction (vector). It differs from distance, which is the total path length (scalar). The answer is 'When force is perpendicular to displacement'. The other options — 'When force equals displacement', 'When the object moves', and 'When there is no friction' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does negative work do to an object?

options.text: Changes its direction without changing speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Negative work → energy is removed from the object (it slows down). Positive work → energy is added to the object (it speeds up). The other options — 'Adds energy', 'Has no effect', and 'Changes its direction without changing speed' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you push a box 5 m with 20 N in the same direction, work done is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Work is done when a force causes displacement: W = F·d·cos(θ). When force and displacement are in the same direction, W = Fd. Work is measured in joules (J). The answer is '100 J'. The other options — '25 J', '4 J', and '200 J' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

Only if the box is heavy

The answer is 'No — gravity is perpendicular to the horizontal displacement'. The other options — 'Yes, gravity always does work', 'Only if the box is heavy', and 'Only if you walk fast' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

options.text: When force and displacement are in the same direction
When force and displacement are perpendicular
When force and displacement are opposite

The answer is 'When force and displacement are in the same direction'. The other options — 'When force and displacement are perpendicular', 'When force and displacement are opposite', and 'Work is always positive' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Kinetic Energy & Potential Energy

Kinetic Energy (KE)

  • The energy of motion: KE = ½mv².
  • Doubles when speed doubles? No — quadruples (KE ∝ v²). Doubling speed quadruples the energy.
  • Always positive or zero (never negative).

Potential Energy (PE)

  • Gravitational PE: Energy due to position in a gravitational field. PE = mgh (near Earth's surface).
  • Elastic PE: Energy stored in a deformed elastic object. PE = ½kx² (spring).
  • Potential energy depends on a reference point — only changes in PE are physically meaningful.

Mechanical Energy

  • Total mechanical energy = KE + PE.
  • When only conservative forces act, mechanical energy is conserved: KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂.
  • A falling ball converts PE to KE. A rising ball converts KE to PE.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for kinetic energy?
quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion: KE = ½mv². The answer is KE = ½mv². The other options — 'KE = mv', 'KE = mgh', and 'KE = ½kx²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If speed doubles, what happens to kinetic energy?
quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion: KE = ½mv². The answer is It quadruples. The other options — 'It doubles', 'It halves', and 'It stays the same' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is gravitational potential energy near Earth's surface?
quiz_questions.explanation: Gravitational potential energy: PE = mgh (mass × gravity × height). The answer is PE = mgh. The other options — 'PE = ½mv²', 'PE = ½kx²', and 'PE = GMm/r' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can kinetic energy be negative?

options.text: No — it is always positive or zero

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion: KE = ½mv². The answer is No — it is always positive or zero. The other options — 'Yes, when moving backward', 'Yes, at very low temperatures', and 'Yes, in space' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is elastic potential energy?
quiz_questions.explanation: Potential energy depends on a reference point — only changes in PE are physically meaningful. Key Concepts — Kinetic Energy & Potential Energy Kinetic Energy (KE) The energy of motion: KE = ½mv². The other options — 'PE = mgh', 'PE = ½mv²', and 'PE = Fd' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: As a ball falls, PE converts to:
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Kinetic Energy & Potential Energy Kinetic Energy (KE) The energy of motion: KE = ½mv². A falling ball converts PE to KE. The other options — 'Thermal energy only', 'Potential energy increases', and 'Nothing' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the KE of a 2 kg object moving at 3 m/s?
quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic energy KE = ½mv². Substitute the given mass and velocity, then calculate. Remember to square the velocity first. The answer is 9 J. The other options — '6 J', '3 J', and '18 J' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Conservation of Energy
summary_sections.content_html:
The Law of Conservation of Energy

Energy cannot be created or destroyed — it can only be transformed from one form to another or transferred between objects.

  • Total energy of an isolated system remains constant.
  • Energy forms include: kinetic, potential (gravitational, elastic, electric), thermal, chemical, nuclear, radiant.

Conservation of Mechanical Energy

  • When only conservative forces (gravity, springs) act: KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂.
  • A pendulum swings back and forth converting KE ↔ PE continuously.
  • A roller coaster: highest point = max PE, lowest point = max KE.

With Non-Conservative Forces

  • Friction converts mechanical energy to thermal energy.
  • Modified equation: KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂ + W_friction.
  • The total energy (all forms) is still conserved.

quiz_questions.question_text: When is mechanical energy conserved?
quiz_questions.explanation: Conservation of Mechanical Energy When only conservative forces (gravity, springs) act: KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂. With Non-Conservative Forces Friction converts mechanical energy to thermal energy. The other options — 'Always', 'Never', and 'Only in collisions' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: At the lowest point of a pendulum, which energy is maximum?
quiz_questions.explanation: Energy forms include — kinetic, potential (gravitational, elastic, electric), thermal, chemical, nuclear, radiant. A roller coaster — highest point = max PE, lowest point = max KE. The other options — 'Potential energy', 'Thermal energy', and 'Elastic energy' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: State the law of conservation of energy.

options.text: Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transformed
Energy can be created from nothing

quiz_questions.explanation: Endangered species face risk of extinction due to habitat loss, overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, and climate change. Conservation efforts include habitat protection, breeding programs, and legislation. The answer is 'Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transformed'. The other options — 'Energy can be created from nothing', 'Energy is always lost', and 'Energy only exists as heat' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a roller coaster, where is speed greatest?
quiz_questions.explanation: A roller coaster — highest point = max PE, lowest point = max KE. The other options — 'At the highest point', 'At the middle', and 'Speed is constant' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to mechanical energy when friction is present?

options.text: Some is converted to thermal energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction opposes relative motion between surfaces. Static friction (f_s ≤ μ_s·N) prevents motion; kinetic friction (f_k = μ_k·N) opposes motion. The answer is 'Some is converted to thermal energy'. The other options — 'It increases', 'It stays the same', and 'It disappears' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Write the conservation of mechanical energy equation.
quiz_questions.explanation: Endangered species face risk of extinction due to habitat loss, overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, and climate change. Conservation efforts include habitat protection, breeding programs, and legislation. The answer is 'KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂'. The other options — 'KE₁ = PE₂', 'KE = PE always', and 'F = ma' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is total energy (all forms) always conserved in an isolated system?
quiz_questions.explanation: The total energy (all forms) is still conserved. Total energy of an isolated system remains constant. The other options — 'No', 'Only in theory', and 'Only at low speeds' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Power & Efficiency

Power

  • Power is the rate of doing work or the rate of energy transfer: P = W/t = ΔE/t.
  • SI unit: Watt (W). 1 W = 1 J/s.
  • Alternative formula: P = Fv (force × velocity) for constant force along the direction of motion.

Efficiency

  • Efficiency measures how much input energy is converted to useful output:
  • η = (useful energy output / total energy input) × 100%.
  • Efficiency is always ≤ 100%. No real machine is 100% efficient due to friction, heat loss, etc.

Examples

  • A 60 W light bulb converts 60 J of electrical energy every second (mostly to heat in incandescent bulbs).
  • A car engine might be 25% efficient — only 25% of fuel energy becomes motion; the rest is lost as heat.
  • LED bulbs are ~90% efficient at converting electrical energy to light.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is power?

options.text: The rate of doing work: P = W/t

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Power & Efficiency Power Power is the rate of doing work or the rate of energy transfer: P = W/t = ΔE/t. The other options — 'The total energy used', 'Force times distance', and 'Mass times acceleration' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of power?
quiz_questions.explanation: SI units: Force = Newton (N = kg·m/s²), Energy/Work = Joule (J = kg·m²/s²), Power = Watt (W = J/s), Momentum = kg·m/s. 1 horsepower ≈ 746 W. The answer is 'Watt (W)'. The other options — 'Joule (J)', 'Newton (N)', and 'Horsepower (hp)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an alternative formula for power?
quiz_questions.explanation: Alternative formula — P = Fv (force × velocity) for constant force along the direction of motion. The other options — 'P = Fd', 'P = mgh', and 'P = ½mv²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can efficiency exceed 100%?
quiz_questions.explanation: No real machine is 100% efficient due to friction, heat loss, etc. Efficiency Efficiency measures how much input energy is converted to useful output :. η = (useful energy output / total energy input) × 100%. The other options — 'Yes, with advanced technology', 'Yes, in quantum mechanics', and 'Yes, with renewable energy' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is efficiency?

options.text: Useful energy output / total energy input × 100%

quiz_questions.explanation: Efficiency Efficiency measures how much input energy is converted to useful output :. η = (useful energy output / total energy input) × 100%. Key Concepts — Power & Efficiency Power Power is the rate of doing work or the rate of energy transfer: P = W/t = ΔE/t.

quiz_questions.question_text: How much power is needed to lift 100 N by 2 m in 4 s?
quiz_questions.explanation: Efficiency Efficiency measures how much input energy is converted to useful output :. η = (useful energy output / total energy input) × 100%. The other options — '200 W', '800 W', and '25 W' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: 1 horsepower is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: SI units: Force = Newton (N = kg·m/s²), Energy/Work = Joule (J = kg·m²/s²), Power = Watt (W = J/s), Momentum = kg·m/s. 1 horsepower ≈ 746 W. The answer is '746 W'. The other options — '1000 W', '100 W', and '500 W' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Work-Energy Theorem
summary_sections.content_html:
The Work-Energy Theorem

The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy:

W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² − ½mu²

  • If net work is positive → object speeds up (KE increases).
  • If net work is negative → object slows down (KE decreases).
  • If net work is zero → speed doesn't change.

Relationship to Newton's Second Law

  • The theorem follows directly from F = ma and kinematics (v² = u² + 2as).
  • W_net = Fd = mad = m(v² − u²)/2 = ΔKE.

Applications

  • Finding braking distance: KE → 0 through friction. W_friction = ΔKE → μmgd = ½mv² → d = v²/(2μg).
  • Determining how much work a variable force does over a distance (area under F-x curve).

quiz_questions.question_text: State the work-energy theorem.

options.text: The net work on an object equals its change in kinetic energy
Power equals work divided by time

quiz_questions.explanation: The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy: W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀². The answer is 'The net work on an object equals its change in kinetic energy'. The other options — 'Work equals force times distance', 'Energy is always conserved', and 'Power equals work divided by time' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If net work is positive, what happens to speed?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Work-Energy Theorem The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² − ½mu² If net work is positive → object speeds up (KE increases). If net work is negative → object slows down (KE decreases). The other options — 'The object slows down', 'Speed stays the same', and 'The object stops' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If net work is zero, what happens to speed?
quiz_questions.explanation: If net work is zero → speed doesn't change. The Work-Energy Theorem The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² − ½mu² If net work is positive → object speeds up (KE increases). The other options — 'The object speeds up', 'The object slows down', and 'The object stops' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Write the work-energy theorem formula.
quiz_questions.explanation: The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy: W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀². The answer is 'W_net = ½mv² − ½mu²'. The other options — 'W = Fd', 'W = mgh', and 'W = ½kx²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 10 N net force moves an object 5 m. Change in KE?
quiz_questions.explanation: A free body diagram shows all forces acting on an object. The net force (vector sum) determines acceleration via F_net = ma. If F_net = 0, the object is in equilibrium. The answer is '50 J'. The other options — '2 J', '15 J', and '500 J' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: For a variable force, what gives the net work?
quiz_questions.explanation: Work is done when a force causes displacement: W = F·d·cos(θ). When force and displacement are in the same direction, W = Fd. Work is measured in joules (J). The answer is 'Area under the F-x graph'. The other options — 'Slope of the F-x graph', 'Height of the F-x graph', and 'The maximum force value' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does the work-energy theorem relate to Newton's Second Law?

options.text: It is derived from F = ma combined with kinematics
It only applies to light objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma. The net force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration. The answer is 'It is derived from F = ma combined with kinematics'. The other options — 'They are unrelated', 'It replaces Newton's laws', and 'It only applies to light objects' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Conservative vs. Non-Conservative Forces
summary_sections.content_html:
Conservative Forces

  • A force is conservative if the work done is independent of the path — it depends only on start and end positions.
  • Examples: gravity, elastic spring force, electrostatic force.
  • Work done in a closed loop by a conservative force = 0.
  • Associated with potential energy: W = −ΔPE.

Non-Conservative Forces

  • Work done depends on the path taken.
  • Examples: friction, air resistance, tension (applied by a person), propulsion force.
  • Friction always does negative work — it converts mechanical energy to thermal energy.

Energy Conservation with Both

  • ΔKE + ΔPE = W_non-conservative.
  • If only conservative forces act: ΔKE + ΔPE = 0 (mechanical energy conserved).
  • If friction is present: some mechanical energy is lost as heat.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a conservative force?

options.text: A force whose work is independent of path
A force that always opposes motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Conservative forces (gravity, spring force, electric force) do work independent of the path taken — only the start and end positions matter. They have associated potential energy. Friction is non-conservative. The answer is 'A force whose work is independent of path'. The other options — 'A force that always opposes motion', 'A force with variable magnitude', and 'Any friction force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of these is a non-conservative force?
quiz_questions.explanation: Conservative forces (gravity, spring force, electric force) do work independent of the path taken — only the start and end positions matter. They have associated potential energy. Friction is non-conservative. The answer is 'Friction'. The other options — 'Gravity', 'Elastic spring force', and 'Electrostatic force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the work done by a conservative force in a closed loop?
quiz_questions.explanation: Conservative forces (gravity, spring force, electric force) do work independent of the path taken — only the start and end positions matter. They have associated potential energy. Friction is non-conservative. The answer is 'Zero'. The other options — 'Maximum', 'Equal to the total energy', and 'Depends on speed' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which type of force is associated with potential energy?
quiz_questions.explanation: Conservative Forces A force is conservative if the work done is independent of the path — it depends only on start and end positions. If only conservative forces act: ΔKE + ΔPE = 0 (mechanical energy conserved). The other options — 'Non-conservative forces', 'All forces', and 'Only gravity' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does friction do positive or negative work?
quiz_questions.explanation: Cations are positive ions (formed by losing electrons); anions are negative ions (formed by gaining electrons). Metals typically form cations; nonmetals form anions. The answer is 'Always negative'. The other options — 'Always positive', 'It depends on direction', and 'Zero' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does friction convert mechanical energy into?
quiz_questions.explanation: Friction opposes relative motion between surfaces. Static friction (f_s ≤ μ_s·N) prevents motion; kinetic friction (f_k = μ_k·N) opposes motion. The answer is 'Thermal energy (heat)'. The other options — 'Kinetic energy', 'Potential energy', and 'Nuclear energy' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If only conservative forces act, ΔKE + ΔPE equals:
quiz_questions.explanation: Conservative forces (gravity, spring force, electric force) do work independent of the path taken — only the start and end positions matter. They have associated potential energy. Friction is non-conservative. The answer is 'Zero'. The other options — 'The total work done', 'The initial KE', and 'Infinity' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Linear Momentum
summary_sections.content_html:
Momentum

  • Linear momentum is the product of mass and velocity: p = mv.
  • SI unit: kg·m/s. Momentum is a vector in the same direction as velocity.
  • A heavy slow object and a light fast object can have the same momentum.

Newton's Second Law (Momentum Form)

  • F = dp/dt — Force equals the rate of change of momentum.
  • For constant mass: F = m(dv/dt) = ma (the familiar form).

Conservation of Momentum

  • In an isolated system (no external forces), total momentum before = total momentum after any interaction.
  • This applies to collisions, explosions, and any internal interaction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is linear momentum?
quiz_questions.explanation: Momentum Linear momentum is the product of mass and velocity: p = mv. The other options — 'p = ma', 'p = Ft', and 'p = ½mv²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of momentum?
quiz_questions.explanation: SI units: Force = Newton (N = kg·m/s²), Energy/Work = Joule (J = kg·m²/s²), Power = Watt (W = J/s), Momentum = kg·m/s. 1 horsepower ≈ 746 W. The answer is 'kg·m/s'. The other options — 'N·m', 'J/s', and 'kg·m/s²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is momentum a scalar or vector?
quiz_questions.explanation: Vectors have both magnitude and direction (velocity, force, acceleration). Scalars have only magnitude (speed, mass, energy). Vectors can be decomposed into perpendicular components. The answer is 'Vector'. The other options — 'Scalar', 'Both', and 'Neither' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: When is total momentum conserved?

options.text: In an isolated system with no net external force

quiz_questions.explanation: Conservation of Momentum In an isolated system (no external forces), total momentum before = total momentum after any interaction. The other options — 'Always', 'Never', and 'Only in elastic collisions' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Newton's Second Law in terms of momentum.
quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma. The net force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration. The answer is 'F = dp/dt'. The other options — 'F = mv', 'F = ma only', and 'F = p/t' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 2 kg object moves at 5 m/s. Its momentum is:
quiz_questions.explanation: A heavy slow object and a light fast object can have the same momentum. The other options — '2.5 kg·m/s', '7 kg·m/s', and '50 kg·m/s' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an explosion from rest, total momentum after is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Conservation of Momentum In an isolated system (no external forces), total momentum before = total momentum after any interaction. The other options — 'Maximum', 'Equal to the energy released', and 'It depends on the fragments' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Impulse
summary_sections.content_html:
What is Impulse?

  • Impulse is the product of force and the time it acts: J = FΔt.
  • SI unit: N·s = kg·m/s (same as momentum).
  • Impulse equals the change in momentum: J = Δp = mv_f − mv_i.

Impulse-Momentum Theorem

FΔt = Δp

  • A large force over a short time produces the same momentum change as a small force over a long time.
  • For variable force: impulse = area under the F-t graph.

Applications

  • Car airbags: Extend the collision time → reduce the force on the passenger.
  • Catching a ball: Moving your hand back extends contact time → less force on your hand.
  • Crumple zones: Car structures designed to deform slowly, increasing Δt and reducing F.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is impulse?

options.text: The product of force and time: J = FΔt

quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse (J) equals the change in momentum: J = FΔt = Δp. A larger force or longer contact time produces a greater impulse. The answer is 'The product of force and time: J = FΔt'. The other options — 'Force divided by time', 'Mass times velocity', and 'Energy times time' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: State the impulse-momentum theorem.
quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse (J) equals the change in momentum: J = FΔt = Δp. A larger force or longer contact time produces a greater impulse. The answer is 'FΔt = Δp'. The other options — 'Ft = mv', 'F = ma', and 'Impulse = KE' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do car airbags reduce injury?

options.text: They extend collision time, reducing average force
They increase the force on the body

quiz_questions.explanation: Applications Car airbags — Extend the collision time → reduce the force on the passenger. The other options — 'They make the car lighter', 'They absorb all the energy', and 'They increase the force on the body' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: For a variable force, how do you find impulse?
quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse (J) equals the change in momentum: J = FΔt = Δp. A larger force or longer contact time produces a greater impulse. The answer is 'Area under the F-t graph'. The other options — 'Slope of the F-t graph', 'Peak of the F-t graph', and 'The average time' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can impulse be negative?

options.text: Yes — if the force is in the negative direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse (J) equals the change in momentum: J = FΔt = Δp. A larger force or longer contact time produces a greater impulse. The answer is 'Yes — if the force is in the negative direction'. The other options — 'No, impulse is always positive', 'Only in explosions', and 'Only for light objects' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do you bend your knees when landing a jump?

options.text: It increases stopping time, reducing force

quiz_questions.explanation: Bending your knees increases the stopping time during a landing. Since impulse J = FΔt = Δp (change in momentum is fixed), increasing Δt reduces the average force on your body. The answer is 'It increases stopping time, reducing force'. The other options — 'It makes you heavier', 'It increases the force', and 'It reduces your momentum' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of impulse?
quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse (J) equals the change in momentum: J = FΔt = Δp. A larger force or longer contact time produces a greater impulse. The answer is 'N·s (same as kg·m/s)'. The other options — 'J', 'W', and 'Pa·s' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Conservation of Momentum
summary_sections.content_html:
The Law of Conservation of Momentum

In an isolated system (no net external force), the total momentum remains constant:

Σp_before = Σp_after

  • m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ (for two objects).
  • Applies in all directions independently (conserve x-momentum and y-momentum separately).

Applications

  • Collisions: Momentum is conserved in all types (elastic, inelastic, perfectly inelastic).
  • Explosions: Total momentum is zero before → total is still zero after (pieces fly in opposite directions).
  • Recoil: When a gun fires, bullet and gun have equal and opposite momenta.

Conditions

  • External forces (friction, gravity) can break conservation — but during short collisions, their effect is often negligible.

quiz_questions.question_text: State the law of conservation of momentum.

options.text: Total momentum before = total momentum after in an isolated system
Momentum is only conserved in elastic collisions

quiz_questions.explanation: Endangered species face risk of extinction due to habitat loss, overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, and climate change. Conservation efforts include habitat protection, breeding programs, and legislation. The answer is 'Total momentum before = total momentum after in an isolated system'. The other options — 'Momentum always increases', 'Momentum is only conserved in elastic collisions', and 'Momentum equals energy' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is momentum conserved in explosions?
quiz_questions.explanation: Applications Collisions — Momentum is conserved in all types (elastic, inelastic, perfectly inelastic). Explosions — Total momentum is zero before → total is still zero after (pieces fly in opposite directions). The other options — 'No', 'Only for symmetric explosions', and 'Only at low speeds' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Write the conservation equation for two objects.
quiz_questions.explanation: Endangered species face risk of extinction due to habitat loss, overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, and climate change. Conservation efforts include habitat protection, breeding programs, and legislation. The answer is 'm₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂'. The other options — 'm₁u₁ = m₂v₂', 'm₁v₁ = m₂v₂', and 'm₁ + m₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a gun recoil, bullet and gun momenta are:

options.text: Equal in magnitude and opposite in direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Recoil — When a gun fires, bullet and gun have equal and opposite momenta. The other options — 'Equal in every way', 'The bullet has more momentum', and 'The gun has more momentum' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is momentum conserved in all types of collisions?
quiz_questions.explanation: In all collisions, total momentum is conserved (in an isolated system). Elastic collisions also conserve kinetic energy; inelastic do not. The answer is 'Yes'. The other options — 'Only elastic', 'Only inelastic', and 'No' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does conservation apply separately to x and y components?

options.text: No — only to the total magnitude

quiz_questions.explanation: Endangered species face risk of extinction due to habitat loss, overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, and climate change. Conservation efforts include habitat protection, breeding programs, and legislation. The answer is 'Yes'. The other options — 'No — only to the total magnitude', 'Only in 1D problems', and 'Only for elastic collisions' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5 kg object at 4 m/s collides and sticks to a 5 kg object at rest. Final velocity?
quiz_questions.explanation: In all collisions, total momentum is conserved (in an isolated system). Elastic collisions also conserve kinetic energy; inelastic do not. The answer is '2 m/s'. The other options — '4 m/s', '0 m/s', and '8 m/s' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Elastic & Inelastic Collisions

Elastic Collisions

  • Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.
  • Objects bounce off each other. No permanent deformation or heat generated.
  • Example: billiard ball collisions (approximately), atomic/molecular collisions.

Inelastic Collisions

  • Momentum is conserved, but kinetic energy is NOT — some KE is converted to heat, sound, or deformation.
  • Most real-world collisions are inelastic.

Perfectly Inelastic Collisions

  • Objects stick together after the collision. Maximum KE is lost.
  • m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = (m₁ + m₂)v_f → v_f = (m₁u₁ + m₂u₂)/(m₁ + m₂).

Comparing Collision Types

  • Elastic: KE conserved, objects separate.
  • Inelastic: KE lost, objects separate.
  • Perfectly inelastic: Maximum KE lost, objects stick.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an elastic collision?

options.text: One where both momentum and KE are conserved
One where momentum is not conserved

quiz_questions.explanation: In an elastic collision, both momentum AND kinetic energy are conserved. Objects bounce off each other. The answer is 'One where both momentum and KE are conserved'. The other options — 'One where objects stick together', 'One where momentum is not conserved', and 'Any collision' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a perfectly inelastic collision?

options.text: Objects stick together — maximum KE lost

quiz_questions.explanation: In an inelastic collision, momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not. In a perfectly inelastic collision, the objects stick together: m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = (m₁+m₂)v_f. The answer is 'Objects stick together — maximum KE lost'. The other options — 'All energy is conserved', 'Objects bounce apart', and 'No collision occurs' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which type of collision loses the most kinetic energy?
quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion: KE = ½mv². The answer is Perfectly inelastic. The other options — 'Elastic', 'Superelastic', and 'They all lose the same' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an elastic head-on collision of equal masses, what happens?
quiz_questions.explanation: In an elastic collision, both momentum AND kinetic energy are conserved. Objects bounce off each other. The answer is 'They exchange velocities'. The other options — 'They stop', 'They both reverse', and 'They stick together' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where does lost KE go in inelastic collisions?
quiz_questions.explanation: In an inelastic collision, momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not. In a perfectly inelastic collision, the objects stick together: m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = (m₁+m₂)v_f. The answer is 'Heat, sound, or deformation'. The other options — 'It disappears', 'Into momentum', and 'Into mass' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Are most real-world collisions elastic or inelastic?
quiz_questions.explanation: In an inelastic collision, momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not. In a perfectly inelastic collision, the objects stick together: m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = (m₁+m₂)v_f. The answer is 'Inelastic'. The other options — 'Elastic', 'Perfectly elastic', and 'Superelastic' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is conserved in ALL types of collisions?
quiz_questions.explanation: In all collisions, total momentum is conserved (in an isolated system). Elastic collisions also conserve kinetic energy; inelastic do not. The answer is 'Momentum'. The other options — 'Kinetic energy', 'Speed', and 'Force' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Center of Mass
summary_sections.content_html:
What is the Center of Mass?

  • The center of mass (COM) is the average position of all mass in a system, weighted by mass.
  • For two particles: x_cm = (m₁x₁ + m₂x₂)/(m₁ + m₂).
  • The COM of a symmetric, uniform object is at its geometric center.

Motion of the Center of Mass

  • The COM moves as if all external forces were applied to a single particle of total mass M at that point.
  • F_ext = Ma_cm — Newton's second law for the center of mass.
  • If no external forces act, the COM moves at constant velocity (or stays at rest).

Applications

  • In an explosion, individual pieces fly apart, but the COM follows the same parabolic path as before the explosion.
  • A high jumper arches their back so their COM passes under the bar even though their body clears it.

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma. The net force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration. The answer is 'F_ext = Ma_cm'. The other options — 'F = mv', 'F = kx', and 'F_ext = mv_cm' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Rocket Propulsion
summary_sections.content_html:
How Rockets Work

  • Rockets propel themselves by expelling mass (exhaust gases) at high velocity — conservation of momentum in action.
  • No external medium is needed — rockets work in the vacuum of space.

The Rocket Equation (Tsiolkovsky)

  • Δv = v_e · ln(m₀/m_f), where v_e is exhaust velocity, m₀ is initial mass, and m_f is final mass.
  • Higher exhaust velocity and larger mass ratio → greater speed change.

Thrust

  • Thrust = v_e · (dm/dt) — the exhaust velocity times the rate of mass ejection.
  • To lift off, thrust must exceed the rocket's weight (mg).

Staging

  • Multi-stage rockets discard empty fuel tanks to reduce mass, dramatically improving efficiency and achievable Δv.

options.text: By expelling exhaust gases — conservation of momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: Momentum (p = mv) is the product of mass and velocity. It is conserved in isolated systems. Greater mass or velocity means greater momentum. The answer is 'By expelling exhaust gases — conservation of momentum'. The other options — 'By pushing against air', 'By magnetic repulsion', and 'By heating the surrounding space' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The other options — 'Thrust = ma only', 'Thrust = mg', and 'Thrust = Fd' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Newton’s Law of Gravitation

Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation

Every mass attracts every other mass with a force:

F = GMm/r²

  • G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg² (gravitational constant).
  • F is the force between masses M and m separated by distance r (center to center).
  • The force is always attractive and follows an inverse-square law.

Key Points

  • Double the distance → force decreases by a factor of 4.
  • The force acts equally on both objects (Newton's third law).
  • Near Earth's surface: F = mg, where g = GM/R² ≈ 9.8 m/s².

quiz_questions.question_text: State Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation.
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Newton’s Law of Gravitation Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation Every mass attracts every other mass with a force: F = GMm/r² G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg² (gravitational constant). The force acts equally on both objects (Newton's third law).

The other options — 'Acceleration due to gravity', 'The weight of 1 kg', and 'The gravitational field strength' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The other options — 'Always repulsive', 'Sometimes repulsive', and 'Neither' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Gravitational Field Strength
summary_sections.content_html:
Gravitational Field

  • A gravitational field exists around every mass. Any other mass placed in the field experiences a force.
  • Gravitational field strength (g): Force per unit mass at a point in the field.
  • g = F/m = GM/r² — units: N/kg or m/s².

Field Lines

  • Field lines point toward the mass (gravity is attractive).
  • Closer lines = stronger field. Lines are radial around a point mass.
  • Near Earth's surface, field lines are approximately parallel (uniform field).

Gravitational Potential Energy

  • Near surface: PE = mgh.
  • General: PE = −GMm/r (negative because work must be done against gravity to move mass to infinity).
  • PE = 0 at infinite separation (by convention).

The answer is 'Because PE = 0 at infinity by convention, and work must be done to reach infinity'. The other options — 'It's a calculation error', 'Gravity repels at close range', and 'Energy is always negative' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Orbital Motion
summary_sections.content_html:
Orbits

  • An orbit occurs when gravity provides exactly the centripetal force needed for circular motion: GMm/r² = mv²/r.
  • Orbital speed: v = √(GM/r) — depends on the central mass M and orbital radius r, not on the orbiting mass.

Orbital Period

  • T = 2πr/v = 2π√(r³/GM).
  • Higher orbit → slower speed but longer period.

Types of Orbits

  • Circular orbit: Constant radius, constant speed.
  • Elliptical orbit: Varying radius and speed (Kepler's first law).
  • Geostationary orbit: T = 24 hours, stays above the same point on Earth. r ≈ 42,164 km from Earth's center.

quiz_questions.question_text: What provides centripetal force for an orbit?
quiz_questions.explanation: For uniform circular motion, the object moves at constant speed in a circle. Centripetal acceleration a_c = v²/r points toward the center. The centripetal force provides this acceleration. The answer is 'Gravity'. The other options — 'Rocket engines', 'Momentum', and 'Inertia' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

options.text: No — only on M and r

quiz_questions.explanation: Electrons fill orbitals following the Aufbau principle (lowest energy first), Pauli exclusion principle (max 2 electrons per orbital), and Hund's rule (fill degenerate orbitals singly before pairing). The answer is 'No — only on M and r'. The other options — 'Yes, heavier objects orbit faster', 'Yes, lighter objects orbit faster', and 'It depends on the altitude only' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for orbital speed?
quiz_questions.explanation: Electrons fill orbitals following the Aufbau principle (lowest energy first), Pauli exclusion principle (max 2 electrons per orbital), and Hund's rule (fill degenerate orbitals singly before pairing). The answer is 'v = √(GM/r)'. The other options — 'v = GM/r', 'v = √(GM·r)', and 'v = 2πr/GM' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Satellite Motion
summary_sections.content_html:
Satellite Fundamentals

  • A satellite stays in orbit because gravity provides centripetal force — it's in continuous free fall around Earth.
  • Orbital speed and period depend only on altitude and the mass of the central body.

Types of Satellite Orbits

  • Low Earth Orbit (LEO): 200–2,000 km altitude. T ≈ 90 min. Used for ISS, imaging.
  • Medium Earth Orbit (MEO): 2,000–35,786 km. Used for GPS satellites.
  • Geostationary Orbit (GEO): 35,786 km altitude. T = 24 hours. Used for communications and weather.

Weightlessness

  • Astronauts on the ISS feel weightless not because there's no gravity, but because they and the station are in free fall together.
  • At ISS altitude (~400 km), g ≈ 8.7 m/s² (about 89% of surface gravity).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Escape Velocity
summary_sections.content_html:
What is Escape Velocity?

  • The minimum speed an object needs to escape a gravitational field without further propulsion.
  • At escape velocity, the object reaches infinity with zero speed (KE → 0 as PE → 0).

Formula

v_esc = √(2GM/r)

  • From Earth's surface: v_esc ≈ 11.2 km/s.
  • Independent of the escaping object's mass.
  • Depends on the mass M and radius r of the body you're escaping from.

Derivation

  • Set total energy = 0 (just barely escaping): ½mv² − GMm/r = 0 → v = √(2GM/r).

Comparison

  • Escape velocity = √2 × orbital velocity at the same radius.
  • Moon: ~2.4 km/s. Jupiter: ~60 km/s. Sun: ~618 km/s.
