summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Kepler’s Laws

Kepler's Three Laws of Planetary Motion

1. Law of Ellipses

  • Every planet orbits the Sun in an ellipse, with the Sun at one focus.
  • Most planetary orbits are nearly circular (low eccentricity).

2. Law of Equal Areas

  • A line from the Sun to a planet sweeps out equal areas in equal times.
  • Planets move faster when closer to the Sun (perihelion) and slower when farther (aphelion).

3. Law of Periods (Harmonic Law)

  • T² ∝ r³ — the square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis.
  • T² = (4π²/GM) r³. This allows calculating one quantity from the other.

Applications: Kepler's third law is used to determine the masses of stars and planets, and to predict orbital parameters of satellites.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Kepler's First Law.

options.text: Planets orbit in ellipses with the Sun at one focus
All orbits have the same period

quiz_questions.explanation: Law of Ellipses Every planet orbits the Sun in an ellipse , with the Sun at one focus. Applications — Kepler's third law is used to determine the masses of stars and planets, and to predict orbital parameters of satellites. The other options — 'Planets orbit in circles', 'All orbits have the same period', and 'Planets move at constant speed' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Kepler's Second Law.

options.text: A line from Sun to planet sweeps equal areas in equal times

quiz_questions.explanation: Law of Equal Areas A line from the Sun to a planet sweeps out equal areas in equal times. Applications — Kepler's third law is used to determine the masses of stars and planets, and to predict orbital parameters of satellites. The other options — 'Planets move at constant speed', 'Closer planets orbit faster', and 'All orbits are circular' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Kepler's Third Law.
quiz_questions.explanation: Law of Periods (Harmonic Law) T² ∝ r³ — the square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis. Applications — Kepler's third law is used to determine the masses of stars and planets, and to predict orbital parameters of satellites.

quiz_questions.question_text: When is a planet fastest in its orbit?
quiz_questions.explanation: For uniform circular motion, the object moves at constant speed in a circle. Centripetal acceleration a_c = v²/r points toward the center. The centripetal force provides this acceleration. The answer is 'At perihelion (closest to Sun)'. The other options — 'At aphelion (farthest from Sun)', 'At all points equally', and 'At the halfway point' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is perihelion?

options.text: The closest point to the Sun in an orbit
The farthest point from the Sun

quiz_questions.explanation: Planets move faster when closer to the Sun (perihelion) and slower when farther (aphelion). 3. The other options — 'The farthest point from the Sun', 'The midpoint of an orbit', and 'The point of zero velocity' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a planet's orbital radius doubles, the period increases by:
quiz_questions.explanation: Electrons fill orbitals following the Aufbau principle (lowest energy first), Pauli exclusion principle (max 2 electrons per orbital), and Hund's rule (fill degenerate orbitals singly before pairing). The answer is 'A factor of 2√2'. The other options — 'A factor of 2', 'A factor of 4', and 'A factor of 8' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Kepler's Third Law as an equation:
quiz_questions.explanation: Applications — Kepler's third law is used to determine the masses of stars and planets, and to predict orbital parameters of satellites. The other options — 'T = 2πr', 'T² = GMr', and 'T = 4π²r³' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)
summary_sections.content_html:
What is SHM?

  • A type of periodic motion where the restoring force is proportional to displacement: F = −kx.
  • The motion repeats in a sinusoidal pattern — described by sine or cosine functions.

Key Equations

  • Position: x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ).
  • Velocity: v(t) = −Aω sin(ωt + φ). Maximum speed = Aω (at equilibrium).
  • Acceleration: a(t) = −Aω² cos(ωt + φ) = −ω²x. Maximum acceleration = Aω² (at extremes).

Examples of SHM

  • Mass on a spring: ω = √(k/m), T = 2π√(m/k).
  • Simple pendulum (small angles): ω = √(g/L), T = 2π√(L/g).

Important Properties

  • Acceleration is always directed toward the equilibrium position.
  • Maximum displacement from equilibrium = amplitude (A).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is simple harmonic motion (SHM)?

options.text: Periodic motion with restoring force proportional to displacement: F = −kx

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line distance from start to finish with direction (vector). It differs from distance, which is the total path length (scalar). The answer is 'Periodic motion with restoring force proportional to displacement: F = −kx'. The other options — 'Any periodic motion', 'Motion in a circle', and 'Motion at constant speed' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the period of a mass-spring system?
quiz_questions.explanation: Hooke's Law: F = -kx, where k is the spring constant and x is displacement from equilibrium. Elastic PE = ½kx². The answer is 'T = 2π√(m/k)'. The other options — 'T = 2π√(k/m)', 'T = mk', and 'T = 2πmk' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where is maximum speed in SHM?

options.text: At the midpoint of one half

quiz_questions.explanation: An object is in equilibrium when the net force is zero (ΣF = 0). Static equilibrium: at rest. Dynamic equilibrium: moving at constant velocity. The answer is 'At the equilibrium position'. The other options — 'At the extremes', 'At the midpoint of one half', and 'Speed is constant' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where is maximum acceleration in SHM?
quiz_questions.explanation: Maximum acceleration = Aω² (at extremes). The other options — 'At equilibrium', 'It's constant everywhere', and 'At the midpoint' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the period of a simple pendulum?
quiz_questions.explanation: The period of a simple pendulum T = 2π√(L/g) depends only on length (L) and gravitational acceleration (g). It does NOT depend on mass or amplitude (for small angles). The answer is 'T = 2π√(L/g)'. The other options — 'T = 2π√(g/L)', 'T = 2πLg', and 'T = L/g' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What direction does acceleration point in SHM?
quiz_questions.explanation: An object is in equilibrium when the net force is zero (ΣF = 0). Static equilibrium: at rest. Dynamic equilibrium: moving at constant velocity. The answer is 'Always toward equilibrium'. The other options — 'Always away from equilibrium', 'In the direction of motion', and 'Perpendicular to motion' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the maximum speed in SHM?
quiz_questions.explanation: Maximum speed = Aω (at equilibrium). The other options — 'v_max = A/ω', 'v_max = Aω²', and 'v_max = ω/A' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Period, Frequency, Amplitude
summary_sections.content_html:
Period (T)

  • The time for one complete cycle of oscillation. SI unit: second (s).
  • For a pendulum: T = 2π√(L/g). For a mass-spring: T = 2π√(m/k).

Frequency (f)

  • The number of complete cycles per second: f = 1/T.
  • SI unit: Hertz (Hz). 1 Hz = 1 cycle per second.
  • Angular frequency: ω = 2πf = 2π/T (rad/s).

Amplitude (A)

  • The maximum displacement from the equilibrium position.
  • Amplitude determines the energy stored: E = ½kA² (spring). Greater amplitude = more energy.
  • Amplitude does NOT affect the period or frequency (in ideal SHM).

Relationships

  • Higher frequency = shorter period = more oscillations per second.
  • Amplitude is independent of frequency/period in SHM.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the period (T) of an oscillation?

options.text: The time for one complete cycle
The number of cycles per second

quiz_questions.explanation: Period (T) The time for one complete cycle of oscillation. The other options — 'The number of cycles per second', 'The maximum displacement', and 'The speed of the oscillation' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: SI unit — Hertz (Hz). 1 Hz = 1 cycle per second. The other options — 'Second (s)', 'Radian (rad)', and 'Newton (N)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does amplitude affect the period of ideal SHM?

options.text: Yes, larger amplitude means longer period
Yes, larger amplitude means shorter period

quiz_questions.explanation: Amplitude does NOT affect the period or frequency (in ideal SHM). Amplitude is independent of frequency/period in SHM. The other options — 'Yes, larger amplitude means longer period', 'Yes, larger amplitude means shorter period', and 'Only for springs' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is angular frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: Angular frequency — ω = 2πf = 2π/T (rad/s). The other options — 'ω = f/2π', 'ω = T/2π', and 'ω = πf' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does the period of a pendulum depend on mass?

options.text: No — only on L and g

quiz_questions.explanation: The period of a simple pendulum T = 2π√(L/g) depends only on length (L) and gravitational acceleration (g). It does NOT depend on mass or amplitude (for small angles). The answer is 'No — only on L and g'. The other options — 'Yes — heavier means longer period', 'Yes — lighter means longer period', and 'Only for very heavy pendulums' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If T = 0.5 s, what is f?
quiz_questions.explanation: The correct answer is '2 Hz'. The other options — '0.5 Hz', '1 Hz', and '4 Hz' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What determines the energy in a spring SHM?
quiz_questions.explanation: Hooke's Law: F = -kx, where k is the spring constant and x is displacement from equilibrium. Elastic PE = ½kx². The answer is 'E = ½kA² (depends on amplitude)'. The other options — 'E = ½mv only', 'E = kA', and 'E = mg' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Energy in SHM
summary_sections.content_html:
Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

  • In SHM, energy continuously converts between kinetic energy and potential energy.
  • Total mechanical energy: E = ½kA² (constant, if no damping).

At Key Positions

  • Equilibrium (x = 0): PE = 0, KE = maximum = ½kA². Speed is maximum.
  • Extremes (x = ±A): KE = 0, PE = maximum = ½kA². Speed is zero.
  • At any position: KE + PE = ½kA² → ½mv² + ½kx² = ½kA².

Graphs

  • KE and PE are both sinusoidal and oscillate at twice the frequency of the motion.
  • When KE is at its peak, PE is at its minimum, and vice versa.
  • Total energy is a horizontal line on an Energy vs. Time graph.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the total energy in undamped SHM?
quiz_questions.explanation: Total mechanical energy — E = ½kA² (constant, if no damping). Total energy is a horizontal line on an Energy vs. The other options — 'E = ½mv²', 'E = mgh', and 'E varies continuously' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: At equilibrium (x = 0), what is the energy distribution?
quiz_questions.explanation: The distributive property: a(b + c) = ab + ac. Multiply each term inside the parentheses by the outside factor. The answer is All kinetic, zero potential. The other options — 'All potential, zero kinetic', 'Equal KE and PE', and 'Zero total energy' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: At the extremes (x = ±A), the energy is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion In SHM, energy continuously converts between kinetic energy and potential energy. The other options — 'All kinetic, zero potential', 'Equal KE and PE', and 'Zero total' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If amplitude doubles, total energy:
quiz_questions.explanation: Total energy is a horizontal line on an Energy vs. Total mechanical energy — E = ½kA² (constant, if no damping). The other options — 'Doubles', 'Stays the same', and 'Halves' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: On an energy-time graph, total energy looks like:
quiz_questions.explanation: Total energy is a horizontal line on an Energy vs. Total mechanical energy — E = ½kA² (constant, if no damping). The other options — 'A sine wave', 'An increasing line', and 'A decreasing line' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: KE and PE oscillate at what frequency compared to the motion?
quiz_questions.explanation: Graphs KE and PE are both sinusoidal and oscillate at twice the frequency of the motion. The other options — 'Same frequency', 'Half the frequency', and 'Three times' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: When KE is at its peak, PE is at its:
quiz_questions.explanation: When KE is at its peak, PE is at its minimum, and vice versa. The other options — 'Maximum', 'Half of total', and 'Equal to KE' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Wave Properties
summary_sections.content_html:
What is a Wave?

  • A wave is a disturbance that transfers energy through a medium (or space) without transferring matter.
  • Transverse waves: Oscillation perpendicular to wave direction (light, water surface waves).
  • Longitudinal waves: Oscillation parallel to wave direction (sound, compression springs).

Key Properties

  • Wavelength (λ): Distance between consecutive identical points (crest to crest). Unit: meters.
  • Frequency (f): Number of waves passing a point per second. Unit: Hz.
  • Speed (v): How fast the wave pattern moves. v = fλ.
  • Amplitude (A): Maximum displacement from rest — determines energy and intensity.

The Wave Equation

  • v = fλ — the fundamental relationship between speed, frequency, and wavelength.
  • Wave speed depends on the medium, not on frequency or amplitude.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a wave?

options.text: A disturbance that transfers energy without transferring matter

quiz_questions.explanation: A wave is a disturbance that transfers energy through a medium (or space) without transferring matter. The other options — 'A movement of matter', 'A type of force', and 'A form of temperature' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a transverse wave?
quiz_questions.explanation: Transverse waves — Oscillation perpendicular to wave direction (light, water surface waves). Longitudinal waves — Oscillation parallel to wave direction (sound, compression springs). The other options — 'Oscillation parallel to wave direction', 'A wave that doesn't move', and 'A wave in a circle' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: State the wave equation.
quiz_questions.explanation: The Wave Equation v = fλ — the fundamental relationship between speed, frequency, and wavelength. Speed (v) : How fast the wave pattern moves. v = fλ. The other options — 'v = f/λ', 'v = λ/f', and 'v = f²λ' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Give an example of a longitudinal wave.
quiz_questions.explanation: Longitudinal waves — Oscillation parallel to wave direction (sound, compression springs). The other options — 'Light', 'Water surface waves', and 'Radio waves' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does wave speed depend on amplitude?

options.text: No — it depends on the medium

quiz_questions.explanation: Wave properties: v = fλ (velocity = frequency × wavelength). Amplitude determines energy/intensity. Frequency and wavelength are inversely related at constant wave speed. The answer is 'No — it depends on the medium'. The other options — 'Yes — larger amplitude means faster', 'Yes — smaller amplitude means faster', and 'It depends on frequency' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If frequency doubles but speed stays the same, wavelength:
quiz_questions.explanation: Wave properties: v = fλ (velocity = frequency × wavelength). Amplitude determines energy/intensity. Frequency and wavelength are inversely related at constant wave speed. The answer is 'Halves'. The other options — 'Doubles', 'Stays the same', and 'Quadruples' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is wavelength (λ)?
quiz_questions.explanation: Wave properties: v = fλ (velocity = frequency × wavelength). Amplitude determines energy/intensity. Frequency and wavelength are inversely related at constant wave speed. The answer is 'Distance between consecutive identical points'. The other options — 'The height of a wave', 'The speed of a wave', and 'The time for one cycle' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Wave Superposition & Interference

Principle of Superposition

  • When two or more waves overlap, the resultant displacement at any point is the sum of the individual displacements.

Constructive Interference

  • Occurs when waves are in phase (crests align with crests).
  • Resultant amplitude = sum of amplitudes.
  • Path difference = 0, λ, 2λ, ... (nλ).

Destructive Interference

  • Occurs when waves are out of phase (crests align with troughs).
  • Resultant amplitude = difference of amplitudes. Complete cancellation if amplitudes are equal.
  • Path difference = λ/2, 3λ/2, ... ((n + ½)λ).

Applications

  • Young's double-slit experiment shows interference fringes (bright and dark bands).
  • Noise-canceling headphones use destructive interference.

quiz_questions.question_text: State the principle of superposition.

options.text: The resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements
Waves pass through each other unchanged

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line distance from start to finish with direction (vector). It differs from distance, which is the total path length (scalar). The answer is 'The resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements'. The other options — 'Waves always cancel', 'Waves always amplify', and 'Waves pass through each other unchanged' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is constructive interference?

options.text: Waves in phase combine to produce larger amplitude

quiz_questions.explanation: Constructive Interference Occurs when waves are in phase (crests align with crests). Destructive Interference Occurs when waves are out of phase (crests align with troughs). The other options — 'Waves cancel each other', 'Waves slow down', and 'Waves change direction' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What path difference gives constructive interference?
quiz_questions.explanation: Constructive Interference Occurs when waves are in phase (crests align with crests). Path difference = 0, λ, 2λ,.. (nλ). The other options — '(n+½)λ', 'nλ/2', and 'Random' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What path difference gives destructive interference?
quiz_questions.explanation: Destructive Interference Occurs when waves are out of phase (crests align with troughs). Path difference = 0, λ, 2λ,.. (nλ). The other options — 'nλ', '2nλ', and '0' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which experiment demonstrated light interference?
quiz_questions.explanation: Applications Young's double-slit experiment shows interference fringes (bright and dark bands). The other options — 'Newton's prism experiment', 'Rutherford's scattering', and 'Michelson-Morley' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do noise-canceling headphones work?
quiz_questions.explanation: Noise-canceling headphones use destructive interference. Destructive Interference Occurs when waves are out of phase (crests align with troughs). The other options — 'Constructive interference', 'Reflection', and 'Refraction' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In complete destructive interference (equal amplitudes), the result is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Resultant amplitude = sum of amplitudes. Resultant amplitude = difference of amplitudes. The other options — 'Double amplitude', 'Half amplitude', and 'Random amplitude' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Standing Waves & Resonance

Standing Waves

  • Formed when two identical waves travel in opposite directions and interfere.
  • They have nodes (zero displacement) and antinodes (maximum displacement) at fixed positions.
  • Energy oscillates between kinetic and potential forms but does not propagate — the wave pattern stands still.

Harmonics

  • Fundamental (1st harmonic): Lowest frequency; L = λ/2 (string fixed at both ends).
  • 2nd harmonic: L = λ. nth harmonic: L = nλ/2.
  • f_n = nf₁ = nv/(2L) for a string fixed at both ends.

Resonance

  • Resonance occurs when a system is driven at its natural frequency — amplitude increases dramatically.
  • Examples: pushing a swing at the right frequency, a wine glass shattering from a matching sound wave.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is a standing wave formed?

options.text: Two identical waves traveling in opposite directions interfere

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Standing Waves & Resonance Standing Waves Formed when two identical waves travel in opposite directions and interfere. The other options — 'A single wave reflects', 'Waves of different frequencies combine', and 'A wave slows down' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are nodes?
quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line distance from start to finish with direction (vector). It differs from distance, which is the total path length (scalar). The answer is 'Points of zero displacement'. The other options — 'Points of maximum displacement', 'The frequency of the wave', and 'The wavelength' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are antinodes?
quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line distance from start to finish with direction (vector). It differs from distance, which is the total path length (scalar). The answer is 'Points of maximum displacement'. The other options — 'Points of zero displacement', 'The speed of the wave', and 'The period of the wave' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does energy propagate in a standing wave?
quiz_questions.explanation: Energy oscillates between kinetic and potential forms but does not propagate — the wave pattern stands still. The other options — 'Yes — all waves carry energy', 'Only at nodes', and 'Only at antinodes' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is resonance?

options.text: When a system is driven at its natural frequency, amplitude increases dramatically

quiz_questions.explanation: Resonance Resonance occurs when a system is driven at its natural frequency — amplitude increases dramatically. The other options — 'When waves cancel', 'When frequency approaches zero', and 'When amplitude decreases' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the nth harmonic of a fixed string, how many nodes?
quiz_questions.explanation: Harmonics Fundamental (1st harmonic) : Lowest frequency; L = λ/2 (string fixed at both ends). 2nd harmonic : L = λ. nth harmonic : L = nλ/2. f_n = nf₁ = nv/(2L) for a string fixed at both ends. They have nodes (zero displacement) and antinodes (maximum displacement) at fixed positions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Fundamental frequency for a string fixed at both ends: L = ?
quiz_questions.explanation: Harmonics Fundamental (1st harmonic) : Lowest frequency; L = λ/2 (string fixed at both ends). 2nd harmonic : L = λ. nth harmonic : L = nλ/2. f_n = nf₁ = nv/(2L) for a string fixed at both ends. The other options — 'λ', '2λ', and 'λ/4' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Doppler Effect
summary_sections.content_html:
The Doppler Effect

  • The change in observed frequency (and wavelength) due to relative motion between a source and observer.

For Sound

  • Source approaching: Wavelength compressed → higher frequency (higher pitch).
  • Source receding: Wavelength stretched → lower frequency (lower pitch).
  • Formula: f' = f × (v ± v_o)/(v ∓ v_s), where v is speed of sound, v_o is observer speed, v_s is source speed.

For Light

  • Blue shift: Light from approaching objects has shorter wavelength (shifted blue).
  • Red shift: Light from receding objects has longer wavelength (shifted red).
  • Astronomical red shift provides evidence that the universe is expanding.

Applications: Radar/speed guns, medical ultrasound, measuring stellar velocities.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Doppler effect?

options.text: Change in observed frequency due to relative motion
Change in amplitude due to distance
Change in wavelength due to temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: The Doppler Effect The change in observed frequency (and wavelength) due to relative motion between a source and observer. The other options — 'Change in amplitude due to distance', 'Change in wavelength due to temperature', and 'Change in speed of sound' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a sound source approaches, pitch:
quiz_questions.explanation: Formula — f' = f × (v ± v_o)/(v ∓ v_s), where v is speed of sound, v_o is observer speed, v_s is source speed. For Sound Source approaching : Wavelength compressed → higher frequency (higher pitch). The other options — 'Decreases', 'Stays the same', and 'Becomes inaudible' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is red shift?

options.text: Light from a receding object shifts to longer wavelength

quiz_questions.explanation: Red shift — Light from receding objects has longer wavelength (shifted red). Astronomical red shift provides evidence that the universe is expanding. The other options — 'Light becomes red in color', 'Light speeds up', and 'Light reflects off red objects' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What evidence does red shift provide?
quiz_questions.explanation: Astronomical red shift provides evidence that the universe is expanding. Red shift — Light from receding objects has longer wavelength (shifted red). The other options — 'The Sun is cooling', 'Stars are made of iron', and 'Light has mass' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does the Doppler effect apply to all types of waves?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Doppler Effect The change in observed frequency (and wavelength) due to relative motion between a source and observer. The other options — 'Only sound', 'Only light', and 'Only water waves' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: When a source approaches, wavelength is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Wave properties: v = fλ (velocity = frequency × wavelength). Amplitude determines energy/intensity. Frequency and wavelength are inversely related at constant wave speed. The answer is 'Compressed'. The other options — 'Stretched', 'Unchanged', and 'Doubled' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is blue shift?

options.text: Light from an approaching object shifts to shorter wavelength

quiz_questions.explanation: For Light Blue shift : Light from approaching objects has shorter wavelength (shifted blue). Red shift — Light from receding objects has longer wavelength (shifted red). The other options — 'Light becomes blue', 'Light slows down', and 'Light refracts through water' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Wave-Particle Duality
summary_sections.content_html:
Wave-Particle Duality

  • Light and matter exhibit both wave-like and particle-like behavior, depending on the experiment.

Light as a Wave

  • Interference, diffraction, and polarization are explained by wave theory.
  • Young's double-slit experiment showed light forms interference fringes — a wave property.

Light as a Particle (Photon)

  • The photoelectric effect showed light ejects electrons as if it were discrete packets (photons).
  • Photon energy: E = hf, where h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant).

Matter Waves (de Broglie)

  • All particles have an associated wavelength: λ = h/p = h/(mv).
  • For macroscopic objects, λ is incredibly tiny (undetectable). For electrons, λ is measurable and significant.
  • Electron diffraction confirmed that particles can behave as waves.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is wave-particle duality?

options.text: Light and matter exhibit both wave and particle behavior
Matter cannot behave as a wave

quiz_questions.explanation: Quantum mechanics describes matter and energy at the atomic scale. Photon energy E = hf. De Broglie wavelength λ = h/mv relates particle momentum to wave behavior. The answer is 'Light and matter exhibit both wave and particle behavior'. The other options — 'Light is only a wave', 'Light is only a particle', and 'Matter cannot behave as a wave' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What experiment showed light as a particle?
quiz_questions.explanation: Light as a Particle (Photon) The photoelectric effect showed light ejects electrons as if it were discrete packets (photons). Young's double-slit experiment showed light forms interference fringes — a wave property. The other options — 'Double-slit experiment', 'Michelson-Morley experiment', and 'Rutherford scattering' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the energy of a photon?
quiz_questions.explanation: Photon energy — E = hf , where h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant). The other options — 'E = mc²', 'E = ½mv²', and 'E = mgh' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the de Broglie wavelength?
quiz_questions.explanation: Wave properties: v = fλ (velocity = frequency × wavelength). Amplitude determines energy/intensity. Frequency and wavelength are inversely related at constant wave speed. The answer is 'λ = h/p = h/(mv)'. The other options — 'λ = mc/h', 'λ = hv/c', and 'λ = mv/h' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What confirmed that particles behave as waves?
quiz_questions.explanation: Electron diffraction confirmed that particles can behave as waves. The other options — 'The photoelectric effect', 'Rutherford's experiment', and 'Newton's prism' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is h called?
quiz_questions.explanation: Photon energy — E = hf , where h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant). The other options — 'Boltzmann's constant', 'Coulomb's constant', and 'The gravitational constant' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Do electrons exhibit interference patterns?
quiz_questions.explanation: Wave-particle duality: electrons (and other particles) exhibit interference patterns in double-slit experiments, confirming they have wave-like properties. The answer is 'Yes'. The other options — 'No', 'Only at high temperatures', and 'Only in metals' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Nature of Sound Waves
summary_sections.content_html:
What is Sound?

  • Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave — it requires a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to travel.
  • Sound cannot travel through a vacuum.
  • Sound waves consist of compressions (high pressure) and rarefactions (low pressure).

Production and Detection

  • Sound is produced by vibrating objects (vocal cords, speakers, tuning forks).
  • Detected by the ear (eardrum vibrates) or by microphones (diaphragm vibrates).

Properties

  • Sound waves can be reflected (echoes), refracted, diffracted, and can interfere.
  • Human hearing range: approximately 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz.
  • Below 20 Hz: infrasound. Above 20,000 Hz: ultrasound.

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of wave is sound?
quiz_questions.explanation: Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave — it requires a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to travel. The other options — 'A transverse electromagnetic wave', 'A transverse mechanical wave', and 'A longitudinal electromagnetic wave' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can sound travel through a vacuum?
quiz_questions.explanation: Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave — it requires a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to travel. Sound cannot travel through a vacuum. The other options — 'Yes', 'Only very loud sounds', and 'Only ultrasound' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What produces sound?
quiz_questions.explanation: Production and Detection Sound is produced by vibrating objects (vocal cords, speakers, tuning forks). The other options — 'Static objects', 'Light sources', and 'Magnetic fields' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the human hearing range?
quiz_questions.explanation: Human hearing range — approximately 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz. The other options — '1 Hz to 1,000 Hz', '100 Hz to 100,000 Hz', and '0 to 10,000 Hz' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are compressions in a sound wave?
quiz_questions.explanation: Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave — it requires a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to travel. Sound waves consist of compressions (high pressure) and rarefactions (low pressure). The other options — 'Low-pressure regions', 'Transverse peaks', and 'Points of zero pressure' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is ultrasound?
quiz_questions.explanation: Above 20,000 Hz: ultrasound. The other options — 'Sound below 20 Hz', 'Very loud sound', and 'Sound from a tuning fork' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can sound be diffracted?
quiz_questions.explanation: Properties Sound waves can be reflected (echoes), refracted, diffracted, and can interfere. The other options — 'No', 'Only at very high frequencies', and 'Only in liquids' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Speed of Sound
summary_sections.content_html:
Speed of Sound

  • In air at 20°C: approximately 343 m/s.
  • Faster in liquids (~1,500 m/s in water) and fastest in solids (~5,000 m/s in steel).
  • Sound travels faster in denser, stiffer media.

Factors Affecting Speed

  • Temperature: Speed increases with temperature. v ≈ 331 + 0.6T (T in °C).
  • Medium: Solids > liquids > gases (generally).
  • Humidity: Moist air carries sound slightly faster than dry air.

The Wave Equation for Sound

  • v = fλ applies. If frequency increases, wavelength decreases (for a given medium).
  • When sound enters a different medium, speed and wavelength change, but frequency stays the same.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the speed of sound in air at 20°C?
quiz_questions.explanation: Speed of Sound In air at 20°C: approximately 343 m/s. Humidity — Moist air carries sound slightly faster than dry air. The other options — 'Approximately 1500 m/s', 'Approximately 100 m/s', and 'Approximately 3 × 10⁸ m/s' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which state of matter is sound fastest?

options.text: Sound speed is the same in all media

quiz_questions.explanation: Faster in liquids (~1,500 m/s in water) and fastest in solids (~5,000 m/s in steel). Medium — Solids > liquids > gases (generally). The other options — 'Gases', 'Liquids', and 'Sound speed is the same in all media' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does temperature affect the speed of sound?
quiz_questions.explanation: Factors Affecting Speed Temperature : Speed increases with temperature. v ≈ 331 + 0.6T (T in °C). Speed of Sound In air at 20°C: approximately 343 m/s. The other options — 'Speed decreases with temperature', 'Temperature has no effect', and 'Speed doubles every 10°C' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: When sound enters a denser medium, what stays the same?
quiz_questions.explanation: When sound enters a different medium, speed and wavelength change, but frequency stays the same. If frequency increases, wavelength decreases (for a given medium). The other options — 'Wavelength', 'Speed', and 'Amplitude' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Give the formula for speed of sound vs. temperature.
quiz_questions.explanation: Factors Affecting Speed Temperature : Speed increases with temperature. v ≈ 331 + 0.6T (T in °C). Speed of Sound In air at 20°C: approximately 343 m/s. The other options — 'v = 343T', 'v = 331T', and 'v = 300 + T' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is sound faster in solids than gases?

options.text: Solids are denser and stiffer, allowing faster energy transfer

quiz_questions.explanation: Sound travels faster in denser, stiffer media. The other options — 'Solids have more air', 'Gases absorb sound', and 'Sound doesn't exist in solids' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Speed of sound in water is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Faster in liquids (~1,500 m/s in water) and fastest in solids (~5,000 m/s in steel). Speed of Sound In air at 20°C: approximately 343 m/s. The other options — '343 m/s', '5,000 m/s', and '100 m/s' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Intensity & Loudness

Sound Intensity

  • Intensity is the power per unit area: I = P/A. SI unit: W/m².
  • Intensity follows an inverse-square law: I ∝ 1/r² (for a point source).
  • Threshold of hearing: ~10⁻¹² W/m². Threshold of pain: ~1 W/m².

Decibel Scale

  • Sound level in decibels: β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀), where I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m².
  • Every increase of 10 dB = 10× intensity. Every increase of 3 dB ≈ 2× intensity.
  • Normal conversation: ~60 dB. Rock concert: ~110 dB. Damage threshold: ~85 dB (prolonged).

Loudness vs Intensity

  • Intensity is a physical measurement (objective).
  • Loudness is a perception (subjective) — depends on frequency, duration, and the listener's hearing.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is sound intensity?

options.text: Power per unit area: I = P/A

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Intensity & Loudness Sound Intensity Intensity is the power per unit area : I = P/A. The other options — 'Frequency of the sound', 'Amplitude of the sound wave', and 'Speed of sound squared' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does intensity vary with distance from a point source?
quiz_questions.explanation: An inverse function f⁻¹(x) 'undoes' the original function. To find it: swap x and y, then solve for y. The answer is 'Inverse-square law: I ∝ 1/r²'. The other options — 'Linearly: I ∝ 1/r', 'It doesn't vary', and 'Exponentially' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the threshold of hearing?
quiz_questions.explanation: Threshold of hearing — ~10⁻¹² W/m². The other options — '~10⁻⁶ W/m²', '~1 W/m²', and '~10⁻³ W/m²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: An increase of 10 dB means intensity increases by:
quiz_questions.explanation: Every increase of 10 dB = 10× intensity. The other options — 'A factor of 2', 'A factor of 100', and 'A factor of 5' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for sound level in decibels?
quiz_questions.explanation: Decibel Scale Sound level in decibels: β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) , where I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m². The other options — 'β = 20 log₁₀(I/I₀)', 'β = I/I₀', and 'β = ln(I/I₀)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: At what dB level can prolonged exposure cause hearing damage?
quiz_questions.explanation: Damage threshold — ~85 dB (prolonged). The other options — 'About 60 dB', 'About 120 dB', and 'About 30 dB' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is normal conversation approximately?
quiz_questions.explanation: Normal conversation — ~60 dB. The other options — 'About 30 dB', 'About 90 dB', and 'About 120 dB' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Pitch & Frequency

Pitch

  • Pitch is the perceived frequency of a sound — higher frequency = higher pitch.
  • Musical notes correspond to specific frequencies. Middle C ≈ 262 Hz. A4 = 440 Hz.

Frequency and Musical Scales

  • An octave = doubling of frequency. A4 = 440 Hz → A5 = 880 Hz.
  • The human ear perceives frequency logarithmically — equal ratios sound like equal intervals.

Timbre (Tone Quality)

  • Two instruments playing the same note sound different because of their harmonic content (overtones).
  • A pure tone is a single frequency (sine wave). Real sounds are combinations of harmonics.
  • The fundamental frequency determines pitch; higher harmonics determine timbre.

quiz_questions.question_text: What determines pitch?
quiz_questions.explanation: The fundamental frequency determines pitch; higher harmonics determine timbre. Key Concepts — Pitch & Frequency Pitch Pitch is the perceived frequency of a sound — higher frequency = higher pitch. The other options — 'Amplitude', 'Wavelength', and 'Speed' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What frequency is concert A (A4)?
quiz_questions.explanation: A4 = 440 Hz → A5 = 880 Hz. The other options — '262 Hz', '880 Hz', and '1000 Hz' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an octave?
quiz_questions.explanation: Frequency and Musical Scales An octave = doubling of frequency. The other options — 'A halving of frequency', 'An increase of 100 Hz', and 'A change in amplitude' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is timbre?

options.text: The tone quality distinguishing different instruments

quiz_questions.explanation: Timbre (Tone Quality) Two instruments playing the same note sound different because of their harmonic content (overtones). The other options — 'The volume of a sound', 'The pitch of a note', and 'The speed of sound' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What determines timbre?

options.text: The mix of harmonics in the sound

quiz_questions.explanation: The fundamental frequency determines pitch; higher harmonics determine timbre. Timbre (Tone Quality) Two instruments playing the same note sound different because of their harmonic content (overtones). The other options — 'Only the fundamental frequency', 'Only the amplitude', and 'The speed of the wave' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does the ear perceive frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: The human ear perceives frequency logarithmically — equal ratios sound like equal intervals. The other options — 'Linearly', 'Exponentially', and 'It doesn't perceive frequency' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A4 is 440 Hz. What is A5?
quiz_questions.explanation: A4 = 440 Hz → A5 = 880 Hz. The other options — '440 Hz', '220 Hz', and '660 Hz' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Resonance in Air Columns
summary_sections.content_html:
Resonance in Tubes

Sound waves in tubes create standing waves at specific resonant frequencies.

Open Tube (both ends open)

  • Antinodes at both ends. Supports all harmonics.
  • f_n = nv/(2L), where n = 1, 2, 3, ...
  • Fundamental wavelength: λ₁ = 2L.

Closed Tube (one end closed)

  • Node at closed end, antinode at open end. Supports only odd harmonics.
  • f_n = nv/(4L), where n = 1, 3, 5, ...
  • Fundamental wavelength: λ₁ = 4L.

Applications

  • Musical instruments: flutes (open), clarinets (effectively closed).
  • Organ pipes produce different pitches based on length and whether they're open or closed.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an open tube, what are at both ends?
quiz_questions.explanation: Open Tube (both ends open) Antinodes at both ends. Closed Tube (one end closed) Node at closed end, antinode at open end. The other options — 'Nodes', 'One node and one antinode', and 'Neither' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What harmonics does a closed tube support?
quiz_questions.explanation: Supports only odd harmonics. f_n = nv/(4L), where n = 1, 3, 5,.. Closed Tube (one end closed) Node at closed end, antinode at open end. The other options — 'All harmonics', 'Only even harmonics', and 'No harmonics' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Formula for resonant frequencies of an open tube?
quiz_questions.explanation: Supports all harmonics. f_n = nv/(2L), where n = 1, 2, 3,.. Resonance in Tubes Sound waves in tubes create standing waves at specific resonant frequencies. The other options — 'f_n = nv/(4L)', 'f_n = 2nv/L', and 'f_n = v/(nL)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the fundamental wavelength for a closed tube?
quiz_questions.explanation: Wave properties: v = fλ (velocity = frequency × wavelength). Amplitude determines energy/intensity. Frequency and wavelength are inversely related at constant wave speed. The answer is 'λ₁ = 4L'. The other options — 'λ₁ = 2L', 'λ₁ = L', and 'λ₁ = L/2' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is a flute an open or closed tube?
quiz_questions.explanation: Closed Tube (one end closed) Node at closed end, antinode at open end. Open Tube (both ends open) Antinodes at both ends. The other options — 'Closed', 'Both', and 'Neither' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a closed tube, what is at the closed end?
quiz_questions.explanation: Closed Tube (one end closed) Node at closed end, antinode at open end. The other options — 'An antinode', 'Nothing', and 'Both' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What harmonics does an open tube support?
quiz_questions.explanation: A parabola y = ax² + bx + c opens upward if a > 0 and downward if a < 0. The leading coefficient determines the direction. The answer is 'All harmonics'. The other options — 'Only odd', 'Only even', and 'None' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Beats & Harmonics

Beats

  • Beats occur when two waves with slightly different frequencies interfere.
  • The result is a periodic variation in loudness (throbbing sound).
  • Beat frequency: f_beat = |f₁ − f₂|.
  • Used in tuning instruments — beats disappear when frequencies match.

Harmonics

  • Harmonics are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency: f₁, 2f₁, 3f₁, ...
  • The 1st harmonic = fundamental. 2nd harmonic = 1st overtone. 3rd harmonic = 2nd overtone.

Harmonic Series

  • Strings and open tubes produce all harmonics.
  • Closed tubes produce only odd harmonics (1st, 3rd, 5th, ...).
  • The mix of harmonics gives each instrument its unique timbre.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are beats?

options.text: Periodic loudness variations when two close frequencies interfere

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Beats & Harmonics Beats Beats occur when two waves with slightly different frequencies interfere. The other options — 'A musical rhythm', 'Sound reflections', and 'Standing waves in air' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is beat frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: Beat frequency = |f₁ - f₂|. When two sources of slightly different frequencies interfere, you hear periodic amplitude variations at the beat frequency. The answer is 'f_beat = |f₁ − f₂|'. The other options — 'f_beat = f₁ + f₂', 'f_beat = f₁ × f₂', and 'f_beat = f₁ / f₂' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How are beats used in tuning instruments?
quiz_questions.explanation: Used in tuning instruments — beats disappear when frequencies match. Key Concepts — Beats & Harmonics Beats Beats occur when two waves with slightly different frequencies interfere. The other options — 'Beats increase when in tune', 'Beats are unrelated to tuning', and 'By counting beats per minute' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 2nd harmonic is also called the:
quiz_questions.explanation: Diploid cells (2n) have two sets of chromosomes (one from each parent). Haploid cells (n) have one set. In humans, diploid = 46 chromosomes, haploid (gametes) = 23. The answer is '1st overtone'. The other options — 'Fundamental', '2nd overtone', and '3rd overtone' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Closed tubes produce what harmonics?
quiz_questions.explanation: Closed tubes produce only odd harmonics (1st, 3rd, 5th,..). Harmonic Series Strings and open tubes produce all harmonics. The other options — 'All harmonics', 'Only even harmonics', and 'No harmonics' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What gives each instrument its unique sound?
quiz_questions.explanation: The mix of harmonics gives each instrument its unique timbre. The other options — 'Only the fundamental frequency', 'The volume', and 'The material of the room' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If two tuning forks have frequencies 440 and 444 Hz, beat frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: Beat frequency = |f₁ - f₂|. When two sources of slightly different frequencies interfere, you hear periodic amplitude variations at the beat frequency. The answer is '4 Hz'. The other options — '884 Hz', '442 Hz', and '2 Hz' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Reflection & Refraction

Reflection

  • Law of Reflection: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection (measured from the normal).
  • Specular reflection: From smooth surfaces (mirrors) — clear image.
  • Diffuse reflection: From rough surfaces — scattered in many directions.

Refraction

  • Light bends when it passes from one medium to another due to a change in speed.
  • Snell's Law: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂.
  • Refractive index: n = c/v (ratio of speed of light in vacuum to speed in the medium).

Key Points

  • Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium (n₂ > n₁).
  • Light bends away from the normal when entering a less dense medium.
  • Frequency is unchanged during refraction; wavelength changes.

quiz_questions.question_text: State the law of reflection.

options.text: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection
Angle of incidence × 2 = angle of reflection

quiz_questions.explanation: A reflection flips a figure over a line (axis of reflection). The image is the same distance from the line as the original, on the opposite side. The answer is Angle of incidence = angle of reflection. The other options — 'Angle of incidence × 2 = angle of reflection', 'Light always reflects at 45°', and 'Reflection doesn't follow laws' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Snell's Law.

options.text: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂
n₁ cos θ₁ = n₂ cos θ₂

quiz_questions.explanation: Snell's Law: n₁sin(θ₁) = n₂sin(θ₂). Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium (higher index of refraction) and away from normal when entering a less dense medium. The answer is 'n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂'. The other options — 'n₁ cos θ₁ = n₂ cos θ₂', 'n₁ θ₁ = n₂ θ₂', and 'n₁/sin θ₁ = n₂/sin θ₂' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the refractive index?
quiz_questions.explanation: Refractive index — n = c/v (ratio of speed of light in vacuum to speed in the medium). The other options — 'n = v/c', 'n = c·v', and 'n = c + v' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: When light enters a denser medium, it bends:
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Points Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium (n₂ > n₁). Light bends away from the normal when entering a less dense medium. The other options — 'Away from the normal', 'Not at all', and 'Parallel to the surface' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does frequency change during refraction?
quiz_questions.explanation: Snell's Law: n₁sin(θ₁) = n₂sin(θ₂). Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium (higher index of refraction) and away from normal when entering a less dense medium. The answer is 'No — wavelength changes'. The other options — 'Yes — frequency changes', 'Both change', and 'Neither changes' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is specular reflection?
quiz_questions.explanation: A reflection flips a figure over a line (axis of reflection). The image is the same distance from the line as the original, on the opposite side. The answer is Reflection from a smooth surface. The other options — 'Reflection from a rough surface', 'Reflection at 90°', and 'No reflection' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Angles of incidence and reflection are measured from:
quiz_questions.explanation: A reflection flips a figure over a line (axis of reflection). The image is the same distance from the line as the original, on the opposite side. The answer is The normal. The other options — 'The surface', 'The horizontal', and 'The vertical' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Lenses & Mirrors

Mirrors

  • Concave mirror: Converging — forms real (inverted) or virtual (upright, magnified) images.
  • Convex mirror: Diverging — always forms virtual, upright, diminished images.
  • Mirror equation: 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i.

Lenses

  • Convex (converging) lens: Positive focal length. Can form real or virtual images.
  • Concave (diverging) lens: Negative focal length. Always forms virtual, upright, diminished images.
  • Thin lens equation: 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i (same form as mirror equation).

Magnification

  • m = −d_i/d_o = h_i/h_o.
  • |m| > 1: enlarged. |m| < 1: diminished. Negative m: inverted.

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of image does a convex mirror always form?
quiz_questions.explanation: Convex mirror — Diverging — always forms virtual, upright, diminished images. Key Concepts — Lenses & Mirrors Mirrors Concave mirror : Converging — forms real (inverted) or virtual (upright, magnified) images. The other options — 'Real and inverted', 'Virtual and enlarged', and 'Real and upright' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: State the mirror/lens equation.
quiz_questions.explanation: Thin lens equation — 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i (same form as mirror equation). The other options — 'f = d_o + d_i', 'f = d_o × d_i', and '1/f = 1/d_o − 1/d_i' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the magnification formula?
quiz_questions.explanation: Cations are positive ions (formed by losing electrons); anions are negative ions (formed by gaining electrons). Metals typically form cations; nonmetals form anions. The answer is 'm = −d_i/d_o'. The other options — 'm = d_i + d_o', 'm = f/d_o', and 'm = d_o/d_i' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If |m| > 1, the image is:
quiz_questions.explanation: The absolute value |x| gives the distance from zero, always non-negative. For equations |expression| = k, split into two cases: expression = k and expression = -k. The answer is Enlarged. The other options — 'Diminished', 'Same size', and 'Virtual' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A diverging lens always forms:
quiz_questions.explanation: Convex mirror — Diverging — always forms virtual, upright, diminished images. Key Concepts — Lenses & Mirrors Mirrors Concave mirror : Converging — forms real (inverted) or virtual (upright, magnified) images. The other options — 'Real, inverted, enlarged image', 'Real, upright image', and 'Virtual, inverted image' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A convex (converging) lens has what kind of focal length?
quiz_questions.explanation: Lenses Convex (converging) lens : Positive focal length. Concave (diverging) lens : Negative focal length. The other options — 'Negative', 'Zero', and 'Infinite' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If m is negative, the image is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Lenses & Mirrors Mirrors Concave mirror : Converging — forms real (inverted) or virtual (upright, magnified) images. The other options — 'Upright', 'Virtual', and 'Larger' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Total Internal Reflection
summary_sections.content_html:
Total Internal Reflection (TIR)

  • Occurs when light travels from a denser to a less dense medium at an angle greater than the critical angle.
  • All light is reflected back — none is refracted.

Critical Angle

  • sin θ_c = n₂/n₁ (where n₁ > n₂).
  • Glass to air: θ_c ≈ 42°. Water to air: θ_c ≈ 49°.
  • At exactly θ_c, the refracted ray travels along the boundary (θ₂ = 90°).

Applications

  • Optical fibers: Light bounces along the fiber by TIR — used in telecommunications and medical endoscopes.
  • Prisms: Binoculars use TIR prisms instead of mirrors for light reflection.
  • Diamonds: Very low critical angle (~24°) causes maximum internal reflection → sparkle.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is total internal reflection (TIR)?

options.text: All light reflected when hitting boundary above the critical angle

quiz_questions.explanation: A reflection flips a figure over a line (axis of reflection). The image is the same distance from the line as the original, on the opposite side. The answer is All light reflected when hitting boundary above the critical angle. The other options — 'Light passing through all materials', 'Light absorbed at a boundary', and 'Partial reflection only' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

Light goes from less dense to denser medium

The other options — 'Light goes from less dense to denser medium', 'At any angle', and 'Only in vacuum' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The other options — 'sin θ_c = n₁/n₂', 'cos θ_c = n₂/n₁', and 'tan θ_c = n₂/n₁' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Optical Instruments
summary_sections.content_html:
The Human Eye

  • The eye uses a converging lens to focus light on the retina.
  • Near point: ~25 cm (closest clear focus). Far point: infinity (for normal vision).
  • Myopia (nearsightedness): Corrected with diverging lens. Hyperopia (farsightedness): Corrected with converging lens.

Magnifying Glass

  • A converging lens held closer than its focal length produces a magnified, virtual, upright image.
  • Angular magnification ≈ 25 cm / f.

Microscope

  • Uses two converging lenses: objective (short f, near object) and eyepiece (longer f).
  • Total magnification = M_objective × M_eyepiece.

Telescope

  • Uses two lenses to view distant objects: objective (large f) creates an image at the focal point of the eyepiece.
  • Angular magnification ≈ f_objective / f_eyepiece.

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of lens does the human eye use?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Human Eye The eye uses a converging lens to focus light on the retina. Magnifying Glass A converging lens held closer than its focal length produces a magnified, virtual, upright image. The other options — 'A diverging lens', 'A flat lens', and 'A prism' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What corrects hyperopia (farsightedness)?

The other options — 'The eyepiece', 'Both are equal', and 'Neither — telescopes use mirrors only' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Cations are positive ions (formed by losing electrons); anions are negative ions (formed by gaining electrons). Metals typically form cations; nonmetals form anions. summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Diffraction & Interference

Diffraction

  • The bending and spreading of waves around obstacles or through openings.
  • Most significant when the opening/obstacle size is comparable to the wavelength.
  • Single-slit diffraction produces a central bright maximum with weaker secondary maxima.

Double-Slit Interference (Young's Experiment)

  • Two coherent sources produce an interference pattern of bright and dark fringes.
  • Bright fringes: d sin θ = nλ (constructive, n = 0, 1, 2, ...).
  • Dark fringes: d sin θ = (n + ½)λ (destructive).
  • Fringe spacing: Δy = λL/d.

Diffraction Gratings

  • Many parallel slits produce very sharp, bright maxima at d sin θ = nλ.
  • Used in spectrometers to separate light into its component wavelengths.

The other options — 'd sin θ = (n+½)λ', 'd cos θ = nλ', and 'd = nλ' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Polarization
summary_sections.content_html:
What is Polarization?

  • Polarization restricts the oscillation direction of a transverse wave to a single plane.
  • Only transverse waves can be polarized — this confirms light is a transverse wave.
  • Unpolarized light oscillates in all directions perpendicular to propagation.

Methods of Polarization

  • Polarizing filters: Absorb one component — only aligned vibrations pass (e.g., Polaroid sunglasses).
  • Reflection: Reflected light is partially polarized. At Brewster's angle, reflected light is fully polarized.
  • Scattering: Scattered light (blue sky) is partially polarized.

Malus's Law

  • When polarized light passes through a second polarizer (analyzer) at angle θ:
  • I = I₀ cos² θ.
  • At θ = 0°: full transmission. At θ = 90°: zero transmission (crossed polarizers).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is polarization?

options.text: Restricting oscillation of a transverse wave to a single plane

quiz_questions.explanation: Polarization restricts light waves to vibrate in a single plane. Polarizing sunglasses block horizontally polarized light (glare from surfaces) while transmitting vertically polarized light. The answer is 'Restricting oscillation of a transverse wave to a single plane'. The other options — 'Increasing the speed of light', 'Changing the color of light', and 'Bending light around obstacles' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

Polarizing sunglasses block horizontally polarized light (glare from surfaces) while transmitting vertically polarized light. The answer is 'No — only transverse waves'. The other options — 'Yes', 'Only in certain media', and 'Only at high frequencies' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

Polarizing sunglasses block horizontally polarized light (glare from surfaces) while transmitting vertically polarized light. The answer is 'That light is a transverse wave'. The other options — 'That light is longitudinal', 'That light has mass', and 'That light is a particle' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Malus's Law When polarized light passes through a second polarizer (analyzer) at angle θ:. The other options — 'I = I₀ sin² θ', 'I = I₀ cos θ', and 'I = I₀/cos² θ' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.explanation: Polarization restricts light waves to vibrate in a single plane.
